q: A startup wants to launch a web application globally without purchasing servers or paying upfront costs. Which cloud benefit enables this?
options: Trade capital expense for variable operational expense with pay-as-you-go pricing
Purchasing physical servers in each target region
Paying a fixed monthly fee for reserved capacity
Signing a 3-year contract for discounted server rates
explain: Cloud computing converts capital expenditure (buying physical servers) into operational expenditure (paying for what you use). This pay-as-you-go model eliminates upfront hardware costs and lets organizations scale spending with actual demand rather than committing to peak capacity.

q: A company's e-commerce site experiences 10x traffic during Black Friday. What cloud benefit allows them to handle this automatically?
options: High availability through multiple data centers
Elasticity — automatically scaling resources up and down based on demand
Global reach through CDN distribution
Economies of scale from shared infrastructure
explain: Elasticity is the cloud's ability to automatically scale resources up during traffic spikes and back down when demand subsides. AWS Auto Scaling continuously adjusts EC2 capacity based on metrics like CPU utilization, so Black Friday peaks are handled automatically without over-provisioning year-round.

q: Which cloud deployment model keeps all infrastructure on-premises with no cloud connectivity?
options: Hybrid cloud combining on-premises and public cloud
Community cloud shared among organizations with similar requirements
Public cloud using shared AWS infrastructure
Private cloud — dedicated infrastructure operated solely for one organization
explain: Private cloud deploys infrastructure exclusively for one organization, either on-premises or in a dedicated hosted facility, with no shared resources. Unlike public cloud, private cloud provides maximum control and isolation but does not connect to public AWS infrastructure.

q: A company uses both AWS for new workloads and keeps legacy apps on-premises. What deployment model is this?
options: Private cloud with on-premises resources only
Pure public cloud using only AWS services
Hybrid cloud combining on-premises and AWS cloud resources
Multi-cloud using multiple public cloud providers
explain: Hybrid cloud integrates on-premises data centers with public cloud services like AWS, enabling organizations to run legacy workloads on-premises while leveraging cloud for new development. AWS Direct Connect and Site-to-Site VPN provide secure, private connectivity between the environments.

q: AWS operates data centers in multiple geographic locations worldwide. What is each distinct geographic location called?
options: Edge Location — CloudFront CDN point of presence
Availability Zone — isolated data center within a region
Local Zone — AWS infrastructure extension near cities
AWS Region — geographic area with multiple availability zones
explain: AWS Regions are geographically distinct locations (us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1) each containing multiple, isolated Availability Zones. Edge Locations are different — they are CloudFront CDN caching endpoints rather than regions with compute capacity.

q: Which AWS concept describes the ability to acquire resources instantly and release them when no longer needed, with no human interaction?
options: Resource pooling sharing infrastructure among multiple customers
Broad network access through internet connectivity
On-demand self-service — provision resources without human intervention
Measured service tracking resource usage for billing
explain: On-demand self-service means users can provision AWS compute, storage, and networking resources instantly via the console, CLI, or API without requiring human assistance from AWS. This is one of the five NIST essential characteristics of cloud computing and enables rapid experimentation and deployment.

q: A company benefits from AWS's massive scale which allows AWS to offer lower prices. What cloud benefit does this represent?
options: Massive economies of scale driving lower pay-as-you-go prices
Global reach deploying in multiple regions
Elasticity scaling resources with demand
Increased speed and agility launching resources in minutes
explain: Because AWS serves hundreds of thousands of customers globally, it achieves economies of scale that continuously lower infrastructure costs. AWS passes these savings to customers through reduced prices — prices have dropped over 100 times since 2006 — providing lower per-unit costs than any single company could achieve independently.

q: A developer can launch a new server on AWS in minutes instead of the weeks required to procure physical hardware. What cloud advantage is this?
options: Economies of scale reducing infrastructure costs
Stop guessing capacity by scaling based on actual demand
Elasticity handling variable workloads efficiently
Increased speed and agility — provision resources in minutes not months
explain: Cloud dramatically increases speed and agility: resources that previously took weeks to procure, ship, rack, and configure now take minutes via API or console. This rapid provisioning allows teams to experiment, iterate, and launch products far faster than on-premises infrastructure allows.

q: Under the AWS shared responsibility model, which of the following is AWS responsible for?
options: Managing IAM users and their permissions
Patching the hypervisor and physical host infrastructure
Configuring security groups on EC2 instances
Encrypting customer data stored in S3
explain: AWS is responsible for 'security OF the cloud': the physical hardware, data center facilities, networking equipment, and hypervisor layer. Customers are responsible for 'security IN the cloud': guest OS patching, application code, data encryption, IAM configurations, and security group rules.

q: Which AWS Support plan provides access to a Technical Account Manager (TAM) and is designed for enterprise customers with mission-critical workloads?
options: Developer Support — business hours email support
Business Support — 24/7 phone and chat support
Enterprise Support — TAM plus proactive guidance
Basic Support — free tier with documentation access
explain: Enterprise Support includes a dedicated Technical Account Manager (TAM) who provides proactive operational guidance, architecture reviews, and helps plan migrations and optimizations. Business Support also provides 24/7 access but lacks the dedicated TAM relationship and Infrastructure Event Management.

q: A company wants to migrate its data center to AWS to eliminate the cost of managing physical hardware. What cloud benefit does this represent?
options: Go global in minutes by deploying in multiple regions
Increase speed and agility through rapid resource provisioning
Trade variable expense for capital expense
Stop spending money running and maintaining data centers
explain: Eliminating data center operations — power, cooling, physical security, hardware refresh cycles, and facility costs — is a distinct cloud benefit. Companies redirect that capital and staff effort toward innovation rather than infrastructure maintenance, achieving both cost savings and organizational focus.

q: Which AWS service provides a unified view of all AWS accounts and enables consolidated billing for an organization?
options: AWS IAM for identity management across accounts
AWS Organizations for multi-account management and consolidated billing
AWS Config for compliance tracking across accounts
AWS Control Tower for account governance automation
explain: AWS Organizations groups multiple AWS accounts under a management account, enabling a single consolidated monthly bill and aggregating usage across accounts to qualify for volume tier pricing. Individual account billing is replaced, and Service Control Policies can be applied across the organization.

q: A company wants to stop guessing how much infrastructure capacity they need. Which cloud benefit addresses this?
options: Stop guessing capacity — scale up or down as actual needs change
Benefit from massive economies of scale reducing fixed costs
Go global in minutes using multiple AWS regions
Trade capital expense for variable expense with pay-as-you-go
explain: With AWS, you scale based on actual demand instead of peak estimates: under-provision and the system can't handle load; over-provision and you waste money on idle capacity. AWS Auto Scaling and on-demand pricing eliminate capacity guessing by automatically adjusting resources to match real-time usage.

q: A retail company operates stores across 50 countries and wants their customers to access content with low latency. Which AWS Global Infrastructure component enables this?
options: AWS Regions for processing data in customer locations
AWS Edge Locations for caching content globally near end users
AWS Local Zones for enterprise applications
AWS Availability Zones for redundancy within each country
explain: AWS Edge Locations (part of Amazon CloudFront) cache content at 400+ Points of Presence globally, serving cached content from the location nearest to end users rather than from origin servers. AWS Regions are separate from Edge Locations — they provide compute capacity, not CDN caching.

q: Which of the following is an example of a managed AWS service where AWS handles patching, scaling, and availability?
options: Amazon RDS — AWS manages database engine, backups, patching, and failover
EC2 with a self-managed Kubernetes cluster
An EC2-hosted application behind an Elastic Load Balancer
EC2 instance where you install and manage your own database
explain: Amazon RDS is a fully managed relational database service where AWS handles OS patching, database engine updates, automated backups, Multi-AZ failover, and read replica management. Installing a database on EC2 requires the customer to manage all of these operations manually.

q: A startup is designing a healthcare platform and wants to understand cloud service models. Which service model provides complete control over the operating system?
options: PaaS — using managed deployment platforms
DaaS — using desktop-as-a-service
SaaS — using a fully managed healthcare application
IaaS — using EC2 to manage OS, middleware, and application
explain: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) provides virtualized compute where customers control the operating system, runtime, middleware, and applications. EC2 exemplifies IaaS — you choose the OS and are responsible for all software management above the hypervisor level.

q: A company needs to choose between deploying in a single large EC2 instance vs many smaller ones. What AWS design principle do multiple smaller instances support?
options: Performance efficiency using the most powerful instance types
Design for failure — assume any component can fail and architect to survive it
Operational excellence through simplified management
Cost optimization through instance consolidation
explain: The AWS Well-Architected Reliability pillar's 'design for failure' principle assumes any component can fail and requires architectures to continue functioning when failures occur. Multiple smaller instances spread across Availability Zones means no single instance failure takes down the application, unlike a single large instance.

q: A company evaluates whether to migrate to AWS. On-premises requires $500,000 upfront hardware + $100,000/year operations. AWS costs $25,000/month. When does AWS become cost-effective?
options: Immediately, because monthly cost is less than annual
After 5 years when both reach equivalent cost
After approximately 2.5 years when cumulative costs become equivalent
After 7 years when cumulative costs are equivalent
explain: TCO break-even analysis: On-premises costs $500,000 upfront + $100,000/year operations. AWS costs $300,000/year ($25,000/month). Setting them equal: $500,000 + $100,000n = $300,000n → n = 2.5 years. After 2.5 years both options have cost $750,000 total; beyond that point, on-premises annual costs are lower.

q: Which pillar of the AWS Well-Architected Framework focuses on running and monitoring systems to deliver business value and continuously improve processes?
options: Reliability — ensuring workload performs correctly consistently
Performance Efficiency — using resources efficiently
Operational Excellence — running and improving operations effectively
Security — protecting information and systems
explain: The Operational Excellence pillar focuses on running and monitoring systems to deliver business value, and continually improving processes. It covers performing operations as code, making small reversible changes, and learning from failures — contrasted with Reliability (recovery), Security (protection), and Performance Efficiency (resource optimization).

q: A company wants to evaluate their AWS architecture against best practices. Which free AWS tool provides this assessment?
options: AWS Trusted Advisor checking against best practices
AWS Inspector scanning for security vulnerabilities
AWS Config evaluating resource configuration compliance
AWS Well-Architected Tool for structured architectural review
explain: The AWS Well-Architected Tool provides a structured questionnaire-based assessment of your architecture against the six Well-Architected pillars, identifies high-risk issues (HRIs), and generates an improvement plan. AWS Trusted Advisor provides automated checks but not the structured architectural narrative the Well-Architected Tool provides.

q: A manufacturing company states 'We no longer maintain our own data centers.' Which cloud benefit is this describing?
options: High availability through global infrastructure and redundancy
Stop spending money running and maintaining data centers
Agility through infrastructure automation and rapid deployment
Elasticity to scale resources automatically based on demand
explain: One of the six key advantages of cloud computing is eliminating data center operations — power, cooling, physical security, and hardware refresh cycles. When a company states it 'no longer maintains its own data centers,' this directly reflects the benefit of stopping spending on running physical infrastructure.

q: What distinguishes a 'serverless' architecture from a traditional server-based one?
options: Serverless uses no computing resources at all
Serverless is only suitable for small applications under 100 users
Serverless requires more configuration than traditional server management
Serverless means you don't provision or manage servers — the cloud provider handles scaling and availability automatically
explain: Serverless computing (AWS Lambda, DynamoDB, API Gateway) means you write code without provisioning or managing servers — AWS automatically handles scaling, patching, and availability. You pay only per invocation or usage with no idle costs, unlike EC2 where instances run 24/7 whether handling requests or not.

q: What is a key characteristic of cloud computing related to how multiple customers use the same physical infrastructure?
options: Measured service billing based on actual consumption
Rapid elasticity scaling resources up and down on demand
Resource pooling serving multiple customers from shared physical resources with multi-tenancy
On-demand self-service with instant provisioning
explain: Resource pooling is a core cloud characteristic where the provider serves multiple customers (tenants) from a shared pool of physical resources, with each customer receiving virtual resources from the common infrastructure. This multi-tenancy model drives economies of scale that reduce per-customer costs.

q: Which AWS program helps customers migrate workloads to AWS by providing credits, training, and technical resources?
options: AWS Free Tier for experimenting with AWS services
AWS Partner Network for technology partner solutions
AWS Marketplace for purchasing third-party software
AWS Migration Acceleration Program (MAP) for enterprise migrations
explain: The AWS Migration Acceleration Program (MAP) provides financial support (credits), operational support (AWS SA engagement), and training to accelerate large-scale enterprise migrations. The AWS Free Tier is for experimentation, not migration support, and AWS Partner Network is for third-party vendors.

q: What is the difference between 'vertical scaling' and 'horizontal scaling' on AWS?
options: Vertical scaling adds more instances; horizontal adds more capacity to one instance
They are identical approaches with different AWS service names
Vertical scaling increases the size/power of one instance; horizontal scaling adds more instances
Vertical scaling is for databases; horizontal scaling is for web servers only
explain: Vertical scaling (scale up) increases the capacity of a single resource — for example, upgrading from a t3.medium to an m5.4xlarge instance. Horizontal scaling (scale out) adds more instances behind a load balancer. Horizontal scaling is preferred for cloud-native architectures because it enables fault tolerance and near-infinite scale.

q: A company wants to test a new idea by spinning up resources for 2 hours, testing, then shutting down with minimal cost. What cloud benefit supports this?
options: Ability to experiment quickly and cheaply — provision, test, then de-provision paying only for 2 hours
Economies of scale reducing long-term infrastructure costs
Global reach for deploying tests in multiple regions
High availability ensuring continuous uptime during testing
explain: Cloud enables cheap, fast experimentation: provision resources in minutes, test, then de-provision — paying only for the time used. Failed experiments can be shut down immediately with minimal cost, compared to on-premises where hardware must be purchased weeks in advance for every test.

q: An e-commerce business uses AWS but maintains legacy systems on-premises for compliance. What cloud deployment model is this?
options: Public cloud with additional public cloud services
Private cloud with AWS-managed dedicated resources
Hybrid cloud combining on-premises infrastructure with AWS public cloud
Multi-cloud using AWS and other cloud providers
explain: Hybrid cloud combines on-premises infrastructure with public cloud services (AWS), connected via Direct Connect or VPN. This allows organizations to maintain legacy workloads or compliance-sensitive data on-premises while leveraging AWS elasticity and services for new development.

q: Which AWS service provides a global network of content delivery that caches content at edge locations near end users?
options: Amazon Route 53 for global DNS routing
Amazon CloudFront CDN for caching content at edge locations
Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration for fast uploads
AWS Global Accelerator for network performance optimization
explain: Amazon CloudFront is a CDN with 400+ edge locations globally that caches static and dynamic content near users, reducing origin server load and dramatically lowering latency. Route 53 is DNS routing, not a CDN, and S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds up uploads rather than caching content near end users.

q: A startup reviews the AWS Well-Architected Framework. What are the main architectural pillars?
options: Cost, Security, Speed, and Compliance
AWS services, features, pricing, and support
Availability, Scalability, Durability, and Redundancy
Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability
explain: The AWS Well-Architected Framework has six pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability. These pillars provide a consistent approach to evaluating architectures and identifying areas for improvement across all AWS workloads.

q: AWS has data centers across the globe. What is the benefit of this global infrastructure for customers?
options: All AWS services are available in every region simultaneously
All data is automatically replicated across all regions
Global infrastructure eliminates the need for disaster recovery planning
Customers can deploy applications globally in minutes to reduce latency for users worldwide
explain: AWS's global infrastructure of 33+ regions enables companies to deploy applications close to users worldwide in minutes, reducing latency and improving user experience. Previously, global infrastructure expansion required years of physical setup — now it requires a few API calls or console clicks.

q: A company wants to control which AWS services their developers can use across all accounts in their organization. Which service enforces this?
options: IAM policies attached to individual user accounts
AWS Organizations Service Control Policies (SCPs) restricting services at the organizational level
AWS CloudTrail logging all service API calls
AWS Config rules monitoring service usage compliance
explain: Service Control Policies (SCPs) in AWS Organizations set permission guardrails at the organizational unit or account level, restricting which AWS services and actions accounts can use. Even root account users in member accounts cannot perform actions blocked by SCPs, enabling centralized governance.

q: Which AWS service allows you to create and manage encryption keys used to protect your data, with full audit trails?
options: AWS Key Management Service (KMS) for creating and managing cryptographic keys
AWS Secrets Manager for storing application credentials
AWS Certificate Manager for SSL/TLS certificates
AWS CloudHSM for dedicated hardware security modules
explain: AWS Key Management Service (KMS) creates and manages Customer Managed Keys (CMKs) used to encrypt data across S3, RDS, EBS, and other services. KMS provides centralized key lifecycle management, automatic annual rotation, IAM-based access control, and full CloudTrail audit logs of every key usage.

q: A company needs to store database passwords and API keys securely with automatic rotation. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?
options: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store for configuration data
AWS Secrets Manager for securely storing and rotating credentials
AWS KMS storing encryption keys
Amazon S3 with server-side encryption for secret files
explain: AWS Secrets Manager stores secrets (database passwords, API keys, OAuth tokens) encrypted with KMS and automatically rotates them on a configurable schedule without application downtime. Systems Manager Parameter Store stores configuration data but does not provide native automatic rotation for database credentials.

q: What is multi-factor authentication (MFA) and why is it critical for AWS root accounts?
options: An additional authentication factor beyond password, critical for root accounts to prevent account takeover
An optional feature only needed for production accounts
A backup authentication method used only when passwords expire
A compliance requirement only for regulated industries
explain: MFA adds a required second authentication factor (TOTP app, hardware key, SMS) beyond the password. AWS root accounts have unrestricted access to all resources and billing — a compromised root account without MFA gives attackers complete control, making MFA mandatory best practice.

q: Which AWS service monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior across your AWS account using ML and threat intelligence?
options: Amazon Inspector scanning for vulnerabilities
AWS Security Hub aggregating security findings
AWS Config tracking configuration changes
Amazon GuardDuty detecting threats using ML and threat intelligence
explain: Amazon GuardDuty analyzes CloudTrail management events, S3 data events, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS query logs using ML and AWS threat intelligence feeds to detect threats like credential compromise, unusual API calls, and cryptocurrency mining. Amazon Inspector scans for software vulnerabilities, not active threats.

q: A company needs to assess their EC2 instances for known vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure. Which service automates this?
options: Amazon GuardDuty for threat detection
AWS Security Hub for compliance dashboard
Amazon Inspector for automated vulnerability assessment of EC2 and containers
AWS Config for configuration compliance checking
explain: Amazon Inspector automatically scans EC2 instances and ECR container images for software vulnerabilities (CVEs from Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures database) and unintended network exposure. GuardDuty detects active threats and anomalous behavior but does not perform vulnerability scanning of software packages.

q: For an EC2 instance, what does the shared responsibility model define regarding management?
options: AWS manages only the physical servers; customer manages everything above
AWS manages everything including applications and data
AWS manages infrastructure; customer manages OS, applications, and data
Customer manages the infrastructure; AWS provides billing and support
explain: For EC2 (IaaS), the shared responsibility model divides duties at the hypervisor: AWS manages physical infrastructure, networking hardware, and the hypervisor. Customers are responsible for the guest operating system (patching), applications, data, IAM configurations, and security group rules.

q: A developer accidentally commits AWS access keys to a public GitHub repository. What should be done immediately?
options: Change the IAM user's password to prevent unauthorized access
Deactivate and delete the exposed access keys immediately, then create new ones
Contact GitHub to remove the committed keys from history
Enable MFA on the account and wait 24 hours for the keys to expire
explain: Exposed AWS access keys must be immediately deactivated and deleted in IAM — automated bots scan public repositories and can begin using exposed keys within seconds. Check CloudTrail for unauthorized usage since exposure, enable MFA on the account, then create new access keys for legitimate applications.

q: Which AWS service provides a centralized security dashboard aggregating findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and other security services?
options: AWS Config for compliance and configuration tracking
Amazon CloudWatch for operational metrics and alarms
Amazon GuardDuty for threat detection aggregation
AWS Security Hub for centralized security posture management
explain: AWS Security Hub aggregates security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and partner tools into a centralized dashboard. It normalizes findings using the AWS Security Finding Format (ASFF) and runs automated compliance checks against CIS, PCI DSS, and other standards.

q: A healthcare provider needs 99.99% uptime for patient records. What cloud architecture characteristic enables this?
options: Global reach to serve patients worldwide
Elasticity to scale capacity during demand peaks
High availability through redundancy across multiple Availability Zones
Economies of scale reducing operational costs
explain: High availability (HA) is achieved by deploying redundant resources across multiple Availability Zones with automated health monitoring and failover. When one AZ experiences a failure, traffic automatically routes to healthy AZs — enabling healthcare systems to maintain 99.99%+ uptime SLAs.

q: Which AWS service enables compliance with regulations by recording all API calls made within your AWS account?
options: Amazon GuardDuty detecting suspicious API usage patterns
AWS CloudTrail logging all API calls for audit and compliance
AWS Config tracking resource configuration changes over time
Amazon CloudWatch for performance monitoring and alerting
explain: AWS CloudTrail records every API call made in your AWS account — who made it (IAM identity), what action was taken, when it occurred, and from which IP. This comprehensive audit trail is essential for security investigations, compliance audits (SOC 2, HIPAA, PCI DSS), and detecting unauthorized actions.

q: A company must comply with PCI DSS for credit card data. Which AWS program provides compliance reports and certifications?
options: AWS Security Hub running PCI DSS compliance checks
AWS Config rules for PCI DSS configuration compliance
AWS Trusted Advisor checking security best practices
AWS Artifact providing on-demand access to AWS compliance reports and certifications
explain: AWS Artifact is a self-service portal providing on-demand access to AWS security and compliance reports (SOC 1/2/3, PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance, ISO 27001/27017/27018 certifications) and compliance agreements. AWS Shield and GuardDuty are security services, not compliance documentation portals.

q: A developer designs microservices where services communicate asynchronously. Why is this 'loosely coupled' architecture beneficial?
options: It automatically encrypts all communication
Services can fail independently without cascading failures and scale independently
It eliminates the need for databases
It reduces the total amount of code required
explain: Loose coupling means services communicate through well-defined interfaces (APIs, queues) rather than direct dependencies. If one service fails or requires maintenance, others continue independently — unlike tightly coupled monolithic applications where one failure cascades to the entire system.

q: Which AWS service protects web applications from common exploits like SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS)?
options: AWS Shield protecting against DDoS attacks
Amazon GuardDuty detecting web-based threats
Amazon Inspector scanning web applications for vulnerabilities
AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) filtering malicious web traffic
explain: AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) inspects HTTP/HTTPS traffic and blocks requests matching rules for OWASP Top 10 attacks including SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS). AWS Shield protects against volumetric DDoS attacks at network layer but does not inspect application-layer web traffic.

q: A company wants to protect against large-scale DDoS attacks at the network and transport layers automatically. Which AWS service provides this for free?
options: AWS Firewall Manager managing WAF rules centrally
AWS Shield Standard protecting all AWS customers automatically at no charge
Amazon GuardDuty detecting volumetric attack patterns
AWS WAF filtering application-layer attacks
explain: AWS Shield Standard is automatically included for all AWS customers at no additional cost, providing always-on detection and protection against the most common network layer (Layer 3) and transport layer (Layer 4) DDoS attacks. Shield Advanced ($3,000/month) adds enhanced detection, DRT access, and cost protection.

q: A DevOps team's application continues functioning when a database node fails by using replicas. What is this capability?
options: Failover — automatically switching to backup infrastructure
Disaster recovery — restoring the entire system from backups
High availability — the system continues operating despite component failures
Redundancy — having duplicate components
explain: High availability means a system continues serving requests even when individual components fail, achieved through redundancy. Using RDS Multi-AZ with automatic failover to a standby replica means the application continues operating even when the primary database node fails, maintaining continuous service.

q: An organization reviews AWS's principle of least privilege for security. What does this mean?
options: Allow everyone to access resources but log all activity
Limit access only to AWS employees
Grant users only the minimum permissions necessary for their jobs
Give all users full administrative access for simplicity
explain: Least privilege means granting only the minimum IAM permissions required for a user or role to perform their specific job — nothing more. If those credentials are compromised, attackers can only access what that account was authorized for, limiting the potential damage of a security breach.

q: A company wants to detect if sensitive data like credit card numbers is stored in their S3 buckets without their knowledge. Which service helps?
options: Amazon Inspector scanning S3 objects for vulnerabilities
AWS Config checking S3 bucket configuration compliance
Amazon Macie using ML to discover and protect sensitive data in S3
Amazon GuardDuty monitoring S3 access patterns
explain: Amazon Macie uses machine learning to automatically discover, classify, and protect sensitive data (PII, financial data, credentials, healthcare records) stored in Amazon S3. GuardDuty detects access anomalies but cannot identify the type of sensitive data contained within S3 objects.

q: Which AWS compliance program specifically addresses healthcare data protection requirements in the United States?
options: PCI DSS for payment card industry compliance
ISO 27001 for information security management
SOC 2 Type II for service organization controls
HIPAA — AWS signs Business Associate Agreements for covered healthcare workloads
explain: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) governs protected health information (PHI) in the United States. AWS signs Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) with healthcare customers, and provides HIPAA-eligible services with appropriate technical and administrative safeguards for PHI workloads.

q: A CFO asks about vendor lock-in with AWS. Which factor best addresses this concern?
options: Customers can export data using standard formats and migrate to other providers
AWS legally guarantees zero switching costs
AWS owns all customer data and can hold it hostage
Vendor lock-in is impossible; all clouds are identical
explain: While migrating from AWS has switching costs (data transfer, refactoring proprietary service dependencies), it is technically possible — customers own their data and can export it in standard formats. The concern is real but not an absolute lock-in; the trade-off between cloud benefits and portability is a business decision.

q: A company wants to centrally manage firewall rules across multiple AWS accounts and VPCs. Which service provides this?
options: AWS Firewall Manager centrally managing security policies across accounts
Amazon Inspector centrally managing vulnerability scanning
AWS WAF managing web application firewall rules per account
AWS Organizations SCPs restricting security service usage
explain: AWS Firewall Manager centrally deploys and manages WAF rules, AWS Shield Advanced protections, Security Groups, and Network Firewall policies across all accounts in AWS Organizations from a single administrative account. Individual account WAF rules cannot be consistently enforced across an organization without Firewall Manager.

q: A European company with GDPR data requirements wants to deploy their application. Where should they use AWS?
options: GDPR compliance is not relevant to AWS infrastructure
Use AWS global content delivery to bypass regional requirements
Deploy in AWS Europe (London, Frankfurt, Stockholm) regions to keep data in Europe
Use AWS US regions and hope GDPR doesn't apply
explain: AWS Regions are geographically distinct locations — eu-west-1 (Ireland), eu-central-1 (Frankfurt), eu-west-2 (London) — that keep data physically within European borders. Data does not leave a Region unless explicitly moved, making regional deployment the mechanism for data residency compliance.

q: Which AWS service provides single sign-on (SSO) access to multiple AWS accounts and business applications?
options: Amazon Cognito for customer-facing application authentication
AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) for unified access to AWS accounts and apps
AWS IAM for identity management across AWS accounts
AWS Directory Service for Active Directory integration
explain: AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) provides centralized single sign-on to multiple AWS accounts and SAML 2.0 business applications (Salesforce, Office 365) using corporate identity providers like Active Directory. Amazon Cognito is for customer-facing application authentication, not employee SSO.

q: A company stores all infrastructure configuration in code (Terraform, CloudFormation). How does this support security?
options: IaC enables consistent security configurations, version control, and peer review of infrastructure changes
IaC eliminates the need for security testing of infrastructure
Infrastructure as Code reduces security risks by eliminating manual console access
IaC automatically encrypts all resources defined in the templates
explain: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with CloudFormation or Terraform enables version control, peer review, and automated testing of infrastructure configurations. Security settings are applied consistently across all deployments, eliminating the human error and configuration drift that occurs with manual console operations.

q: A media company designing a video platform requires compute, storage, and database. Which service model provides infrastructure control with managed services?
options: PaaS — using RDS, S3, and serverless for less operations
DaaS — using desktop-as-a-service
Pure SaaS — using only fully managed third-party applications
IaaS — using EC2 and S3 with self-managed databases
explain: PaaS (Platform as a Service) provides managed services like Amazon RDS, S3, and Lambda that eliminate infrastructure operations while developers retain control of application logic and data. IaaS (EC2 with self-managed databases) provides more granular control but requires significantly more operational overhead.

q: Which AWS service provides dedicated, single-tenant hardware security modules (HSMs) for generating and using your own encryption keys?
options: AWS Certificate Manager for SSL/TLS certificate management
AWS KMS using shared multi-tenant hardware
AWS CloudHSM with dedicated single-tenant HSM hardware
AWS Secrets Manager for application credential storage
explain: AWS CloudHSM provides dedicated, single-tenant Hardware Security Module (HSM) appliances that meet FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certification requirements. Unlike KMS (multi-tenant shared hardware), CloudHSM gives customers exclusive control of the HSM and cryptographic keys — required when regulations mandate dedicated hardware.

q: A company receives an AWS abuse notification that their EC2 instance is sending spam emails. What should they do first?
options: Ignore it since AWS may have made an error in detection
Immediately investigate the instance for compromise, isolate it, and respond to AWS abuse team
Terminate the instance immediately and file an insurance claim
Change the security group to block all outbound traffic and continue operations
explain: AWS abuse notifications should be treated as high-priority security incidents. Immediately isolate the instance (modify security group to deny all traffic), take a forensic snapshot, investigate for malware or unauthorized processes, and respond to abuse@amazonaws.com within 24 hours with your remediation steps.

q: What is the AWS Shared Responsibility Model's customer responsibility for Amazon RDS?
options: Patching the database engine software and OS
Ensuring RDS Multi-AZ failover works correctly
Managing database schema, data encryption, and access control (IAM, security groups)
Maintaining physical hardware and hypervisor for RDS instances
explain: For Amazon RDS, AWS manages the database engine patching, OS patching, Multi-AZ failover, automated backups, and underlying hardware. Customers are responsible for database schema design, application-level access control (database user accounts and permissions), data encryption settings, and security group configuration.

q: Which of the following is a customer's responsibility under the shared responsibility model for Amazon EC2?
options: Ensuring the hypervisor is secure and properly isolated between instances
Maintaining the physical host servers and network hardware
Patching the operating system, managing applications, and configuring security groups
Providing power, cooling, and physical security for the data center
explain: EC2 is IaaS — customers own the guest OS layer and everything above it. This includes applying OS security patches, managing application software, configuring security groups and NACLs, managing IAM access credentials, and encrypting data. AWS manages the hypervisor and physical infrastructure.

q: A company wants to encrypt data in transit between their application and an RDS database. What should they enable?
options: AWS KMS encryption keys for the RDS volume
S3 server-side encryption for data stored in the database
SSL/TLS encryption for connections between the application and RDS endpoint
VPC peering to prevent traffic from traveling over the public internet
explain: Encryption in transit (TLS/SSL) protects data as it moves between the application and RDS endpoint over the network. RDS provides TLS certificates and applications configure SSL connection strings to use them. VPC isolation keeps traffic off the public internet but does not encrypt the data within the connection.

q: An architect explains: 'We pay for compute resources by the second when running.' Which cloud pricing benefit is this?
options: Consolidated billing discounts
Pay-as-you-go model charging only for consumed resources
Spot instances for always-on workloads
Reserved pricing forcing long-term commitments
explain: Pay-as-you-go pricing charges for resources based on actual consumption — EC2 per second, S3 per GB, Lambda per invocation. There are no upfront fees or minimum commitments in the On-Demand model, making it ideal for variable workloads and eliminating wasted spend on idle capacity.

q: Which AWS service provides a managed, scalable solution for DDoS protection with 24/7 access to the DDoS Response Team?
options: Amazon CloudFront absorbing volumetric DDoS traffic at edge
AWS Shield Advanced for enhanced DDoS protection with DRT access
AWS WAF filtering application-layer DDoS attacks
AWS Shield Standard included automatically for all customers
explain: AWS Shield Advanced ($3,000/month) provides enhanced DDoS detection, near real-time attack visibility in CloudWatch, 24/7 access to the AWS DDoS Response Team (DRT), and cost protection credits for scaling charges incurred during DDoS attacks. Shield Standard provides basic protection at no cost.

q: A company wants to ensure only their own encryption keys protect their S3 data, with AWS never having access to the plaintext keys. What option achieves this?
options: SSE-KMS using Customer Managed Keys in AWS KMS
SSE-C — customer-provided encryption keys where the customer controls and stores keys outside AWS
S3 Object Lock preventing key access to AWS
SSE-S3 using AWS-managed keys automatically
explain: SSE-C (Server-Side Encryption with Customer-Provided Keys) allows customers to provide their own encryption keys with each API request to S3. AWS performs the encryption/decryption but never stores the key — it is used and immediately discarded. SSE-KMS uses AWS KMS where AWS does store and manage the key.

q: What AWS feature allows you to define and enforce which regions AWS resources can be created in?
options: IAM policies with region conditions restricting service access
AWS Organizations SCPs with region restriction conditions
Both IAM policies and SCPs can enforce region restrictions
AWS Config rules monitoring resource regions
explain: Region restrictions can be enforced using both IAM condition keys (aws:RequestedRegion in identity-based policies) and SCPs (aws:RequestedRegion condition in organizational policies). SCPs provide the stronger enforcement because they apply organization-wide even to account administrators.

q: A nonprofit mentions: 'We focus on our mission instead of managing servers.' Which benefit are they describing?
options: Global reach across regions
Agility — offloading infrastructure management to AWS
High availability through redundancy
Elasticity to handle traffic spikes
explain: Agility in cloud computing means rapid access to a wide range of technologies, infrastructure, and services — allowing teams to experiment and innovate faster. When organizations focus on their core mission rather than managing servers, this represents the agility benefit of cloud infrastructure abstraction.

q: Which AWS service integrates with Microsoft Active Directory to provide directory services in the AWS cloud?
options: AWS IAM Identity Center replacing Active Directory
AWS Directory Service providing managed Active Directory in AWS
AWS Organizations for multi-account directory management
Amazon Cognito for enterprise directory integration
explain: AWS Directory Service provides managed Microsoft Active Directory (AWS Managed Microsoft AD), Simple AD for basic LDAP, and AD Connector to proxy authentication to an on-premises Active Directory. This enables EC2 instances to join a domain and employees to use Windows credentials for AWS resources.

q: A company needs to meet GDPR requirements for data stored in AWS. What is their primary responsibility?
options: AWS Artifact provides GDPR compliance that covers customer data automatically
The customer is responsible for data classification, access controls, processing purposes, and data subject rights management
GDPR only applies to European AWS regions, not global deployments
AWS is fully responsible for GDPR compliance for all customer data
explain: Under GDPR, AWS acts as a data processor and customers act as data controllers — meaning customers are responsible for what personal data they collect, how they process it, the legal basis for processing, data retention periods, and responding to data subject rights requests (access, deletion, portability).

q: Comparing cloud deployment models, which is shared by multiple organizations but not the public?
options: Public cloud — shared by all
Community cloud — shared by organizations with similar requirements
Hybrid cloud — on-premises plus public cloud
Private cloud — single organization only
explain: Community cloud is shared among organizations with similar requirements (same industry vertical, regulatory compliance needs, or government agency type) but is not open to the general public. It sits between private cloud (single org) and public cloud (anyone) in terms of sharing and cost.

q: A company needs a scalable object storage solution for storing millions of images with 99.999999999% durability. Which service should they use?
options: Amazon EFS elastic file system for shared storage
Amazon S3 object storage with eleven 9s of durability
Amazon EBS block storage attached to EC2 instances
Amazon Glacier for long-term archival storage
explain: Amazon S3 stores data redundantly across a minimum of three Availability Zones within a region, providing 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability — meaning losing an object is statistically equivalent to 1 in 10 trillion. EBS and EFS have lower durability targets and are designed for different access patterns.

q: A company wants to run a web server without managing the operating system, patching, or scaling. Which AWS service best fits?
options: Amazon ECS running containers on managed EC2 clusters
Amazon Lightsail for simple VPS management
Amazon EC2 with auto-managed OS patching
AWS Elastic Beanstalk deploying applications without managing underlying infrastructure
explain: AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a PaaS that automatically handles deployment infrastructure: provisioning EC2 instances, load balancers, and Auto Scaling groups; deploying application code; and monitoring health. Developers upload code packages and Beanstalk manages the underlying infrastructure.

q: Which EC2 pricing model offers up to 90% discount for fault-tolerant workloads that can tolerate interruption?
options: Spot Instances using spare EC2 capacity at discounted prices
On-Demand Instances with per-second billing
Reserved Instances with 1 or 3-year commitment
Dedicated Hosts for compliance and licensing requirements
explain: EC2 Spot Instances purchase spare AWS capacity at up to 90% discount over On-Demand prices. AWS reclaims Spot Instances with a 2-minute warning when capacity is needed elsewhere, making them suitable only for fault-tolerant, interruption-tolerant workloads like batch processing and rendering.

q: A company runs a database that requires consistent compute performance 24/7 for the next 3 years. Which pricing model provides the lowest cost?
options: Spot Instances for maximum discount
Savings Plans with compute flexibility
Reserved Instances with 3-year All Upfront payment
On-Demand Instances for maximum flexibility
explain: Reserved Instances with 3-year All Upfront commitment provide the maximum possible EC2 discount — up to 72% off On-Demand pricing. For a database requiring 24/7 compute over 3 years, this commitment model dramatically outperforms On-Demand and even Savings Plans in total cost.

q: Which AWS service provides managed relational databases with automated backups, Multi-AZ failover, and no OS management?
options: Amazon Aurora for cloud-native relational databases only
Amazon DynamoDB for NoSQL database management
Amazon RDS for managed relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, etc.)
Amazon Redshift for data warehousing
explain: Amazon RDS manages six relational database engines (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Aurora) with automated backups, point-in-time recovery, Multi-AZ high availability, and automated OS and engine patching. Customers do not need to manage EC2 instances, storage, or database software installation.

q: A startup needs a NoSQL database that can handle millions of requests per second with single-digit millisecond latency. Which service is best?
options: Amazon RDS MySQL with read replicas for scaling
Amazon Neptune for graph database workloads
Amazon DynamoDB fully managed NoSQL with consistent single-digit ms performance
Amazon ElastiCache Redis for in-memory caching
explain: Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed serverless NoSQL database that delivers consistent single-digit millisecond response times at any scale, from startup to millions of requests per second. Unlike RDS (which requires read replica configuration for scaling), DynamoDB scales automatically with no capacity planning required.

q: A company reviews AWS Availability Zones. What is the primary purpose of AZs in AWS regions?
options: AZs replace the need for backups
Each region has only one AZ
AZs are separate data centers within a region enabling high availability and disaster recovery
AZs are geographically distant to serve customers worldwide
explain: AWS Availability Zones are one or more physically separated, redundant data centers within a region — each with independent power, cooling, and networking. Deploying across multiple AZs ensures that a data center failure in one AZ does not affect applications running in other AZs.

q: A company needs to distribute HTTP/HTTPS traffic across multiple EC2 instances with health checks and SSL termination. Which service provides this?
options: Amazon Route 53 for DNS-based load balancing
Application Load Balancer (ALB) for Layer 7 HTTP/HTTPS routing
Network Load Balancer (NLB) for ultra-low latency TCP/UDP
Classic Load Balancer for legacy EC2-Classic workloads
explain: Application Load Balancer (ALB) operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS), enabling path-based routing (/api vs /static), host-based routing (api.example.com vs app.example.com), SSL/TLS termination, WebSocket support, and integration with AWS WAF. Network Load Balancer (NLB) operates at Layer 4 for ultra-low latency TCP/UDP.

q: A company discusses cloud benefits and mentions 'infrastructure scales automatically during peak usage.' Which benefit is this?
options: Elasticity — automatically scaling resources based on demand
Cost optimization through reserved capacity
Global reach through multiple regions
Economies of scale from bulk purchasing
explain: Elasticity describes the automatic scaling of cloud resources up or down in response to demand changes. During a traffic spike, AWS Auto Scaling launches additional EC2 instances; during low traffic periods, it terminates unnecessary instances — the customer pays only for active capacity.

q: Which S3 storage class is designed for infrequently accessed data that still needs millisecond retrieval, at lower cost than S3 Standard?
options: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval for archival with 1-12 hour retrieval
S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moving data between tiers
S3 Standard-IA (Infrequent Access) for less frequent access with instant retrieval
S3 Standard for frequently accessed data
explain: S3 Standard-IA (Infrequent Access) offers the same 11-nines durability and millisecond retrieval as S3 Standard but charges a lower per-GB storage price plus a per-GB retrieval fee. It's ideal for disaster recovery data or backups accessed monthly — S3 Glacier requires minutes-to-hours retrieval time.

q: A company wants to run containerized applications without managing EC2 instances or clusters. Which service provides serverless containers?
options: AWS Fargate running containers without managing underlying servers
AWS Batch for containerized batch processing jobs
Amazon EKS managing Kubernetes control plane only
Amazon ECS on EC2 launch type managing your own cluster
explain: AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers that removes the need to provision, configure, or manage EC2 instances. You define container CPU and memory requirements and Fargate handles the underlying infrastructure, OS security patches, and auto-scaling — unlike ECS on EC2 where you manage cluster nodes.

q: Which AWS service provides a petabyte-scale data warehouse for running complex analytical queries?
options: Amazon Aurora for fast cloud-native database queries
Amazon RDS for transactional relational database queries
Amazon DynamoDB for NoSQL analytical workloads
Amazon Redshift for petabyte-scale data warehousing and analytics
explain: Amazon Redshift is a fully managed data warehouse using columnar storage and massively parallel processing (MPP) to query petabytes of structured data. Unlike Amazon RDS (optimized for OLTP transactions), Redshift is optimized for OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) with complex aggregations and joins across large datasets.

q: Reviewing cloud service models, which provides the most control to the customer?
options: Managed services — AWS manages everything
IaaS — maximum control; customer manages OS, middleware, and applications
PaaS — some control over applications and data
SaaS — no control, fully managed applications
explain: IaaS provides the most control: with EC2, customers manage the guest OS, middleware, runtime, and application. PaaS (Elastic Beanstalk, RDS) abstracts the infrastructure but limits OS-level control. SaaS (Salesforce, Workday) provides the least control — only data and application configuration.

q: A company wants to run their application code without any server management and pay only when their code executes. This describes which model?
options: CaaS — Containers as a Service on managed Kubernetes
Serverless computing using Lambda for event-driven execution
PaaS — Platform as a Service with managed runtime
IaaS — Infrastructure as a Service with managed VMs
explain: Serverless computing (AWS Lambda) runs code in response to events without provisioning servers — Lambda scales automatically from zero to thousands of concurrent executions. You pay per 1ms of execution time rather than for idle server capacity, making it cost-effective for event-driven and variable workloads.

q: Which AWS service provides a fully managed message queue for decoupling application components?
options: Amazon Kinesis for real-time streaming data processing
Amazon SQS for reliable message queuing between services
Amazon EventBridge for event-driven architecture routing
Amazon SNS for publish/subscribe notifications
explain: Amazon SQS provides managed message queues that store messages until consumers process them. This decouples producers from consumers — the order service can write to SQS without knowing whether the inventory service is available, enabling independent scaling and resilience in microservices architectures.

q: A company wants to send notifications to millions of subscribers across email, SMS, and mobile push simultaneously. Which service enables this?
options: Amazon SQS for reliable message delivery to queues
Amazon SNS for pub/sub notifications to multiple subscribers across multiple protocols
Amazon SES for transactional email delivery
Amazon Pinpoint for targeted customer communication campaigns
explain: Amazon SNS is a managed pub/sub messaging service that fans out a single message to multiple subscribers simultaneously: email, SMS, HTTP endpoints, SQS queues, Lambda functions, and mobile push notifications. SQS queues store messages for later consumption — SNS delivers to all subscribers immediately.

q: A company evaluates cloud resilience. Which architectural approach provides the highest reliability?
options: Single data center for simplicity
Manual failover between regions
Backup-only approach
Redundancy across multiple AZs with automated failover
explain: Deploying resources across multiple Availability Zones with automated failover (via ELB and Auto Scaling or RDS Multi-AZ) is the foundational AWS pattern for high reliability. A single data center deployment or manual failover cannot match the automated recovery speed of multi-AZ architectures.

q: Which AWS service provides managed Kubernetes for running containerized applications?
options: AWS App Runner for fully managed containerized web apps
Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) for managed Kubernetes clusters
Amazon ECS for AWS-native container orchestration
AWS Fargate for serverless container execution
explain: Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) manages the Kubernetes control plane (API server, etcd cluster) across multiple AZs, handling Kubernetes version upgrades and control plane patching. Customers manage worker nodes (on EC2 or Fargate) and deploy standard Kubernetes workloads using kubectl and Helm.

q: A company needs to migrate a large amount of data (100TB) from on-premises to S3 but has limited internet bandwidth. Which service helps?
options: AWS DataSync for network-based transfer optimization
AWS Snowball Edge physical device for offline data migration
AWS Direct Connect providing dedicated 10Gbps connection
Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration for faster uploads
explain: AWS Snowball Edge is a physical device that holds up to 80TB of data. You copy data to it on-premises and ship it back to AWS, which loads data into S3 — faster and cheaper than internet transfer for large datasets. For 100TB with limited bandwidth, internet transfer would take weeks or months.

q: Which AWS service provides dedicated, high-bandwidth private network connectivity between on-premises and AWS?
options: AWS Direct Connect providing dedicated private network connections
Amazon Route 53 for DNS-based routing to AWS
AWS VPN using encrypted tunnels over the internet
AWS Transit Gateway connecting VPCs and on-premises networks
explain: AWS Direct Connect establishes a dedicated private fiber connection from a customer's data center to AWS, bypassing the public internet. This provides consistent network performance (no internet congestion), lower latency, predictable bandwidth, and reduced data transfer costs compared to internet-based connections.

q: A company discusses AWS regions for their application deployment. Why would they deploy across multiple regions?
options: All regions are identical so region choice doesn't matter
Single region reduces costs
Multiple regions provide disaster recovery, low latency for global users, and regulatory compliance
Regulations prohibit multi-region deployment
explain: Multi-region deployment provides: (1) disaster recovery — if one region fails, traffic routes to another; (2) low latency — serve users from the geographically nearest region; and (3) data residency compliance — keep data in required jurisdictions. Single-region deployment has lower cost but no geographic redundancy.

q: A financial services company must comply with PCI DSS. Which AWS service provides compliance reports?
options: AWS Shield for DDoS protection
AWS Artifact for accessing compliance reports and certifications
AWS GuardDuty for threat detection
AWS KMS for encryption
explain: AWS Artifact provides a self-service portal for downloading AWS compliance reports (SOC 2 Type II, PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance, ISO 27001/27017/27018) and signing compliance agreements (HIPAA BAA, GDPR DPA). It does not provide active threat detection or security recommendations.

q: A company runs a critical application on EC2 and wants to ensure it continues running even if an entire AWS Availability Zone fails. What should they implement?
options: Use a larger EC2 instance type with more redundant hardware
Deploy EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones behind a load balancer
Enable EC2 instance recovery for automatic restart after failure
Use Reserved Instances for guaranteed instance availability
explain: Deploying EC2 instances across multiple AZs behind an Elastic Load Balancer ensures that if one entire AZ fails, the ELB routes traffic only to healthy instances in the remaining AZs. Larger instance types only add more compute in a single failure domain and don't protect against AZ-level failures.

q: Which AWS service is a managed, serverless data integration service for discovering, preparing, and combining data for analytics?
options: Amazon Kinesis for real-time streaming data ingestion
AWS Glue for serverless ETL and data catalog
Amazon EMR for big data processing with Spark and Hadoop
Amazon Redshift for data warehousing workloads
explain: AWS Glue is a serverless ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) service with a centralized Data Catalog for discovering and organizing data across S3, RDS, Redshift, and other sources. Unlike Amazon EMR (requires cluster management), Glue requires no server provisioning — pay only for processing time during job runs.

q: A DevOps engineer configures MFA for IAM users. Why is MFA critical for AWS security?
options: It eliminates the need for passwords
It requires a second verification beyond the password, preventing account compromise if passwords leak
It encrypts all network traffic
It automatically backs up user accounts
explain: MFA requires a second authentication factor (TOTP authenticator app, hardware security key, or SMS) in addition to the password. Even if an attacker obtains a user's password through phishing or data breach, they cannot access the account without the second factor, dramatically reducing account compromise risk.

q: A company needs to protect their web application from SQL injection and XSS attacks. Which service should they use?
options: AWS GuardDuty — detecting intrusions
AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) — filtering malicious requests
AWS Shield — protecting against DDoS attacks
AWS KMS — managing encryption keys
explain: AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests and blocks traffic matching rules for common web exploits (OWASP Top 10): SQL injection attempts in query strings, cross-site scripting (XSS) in request headers, and malicious bot signatures. It integrates with CloudFront, ALB, and API Gateway.

q: Which AWS service provides a managed Apache Kafka-compatible message streaming service?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams for real-time streaming
Amazon SQS for reliable message queue processing
Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka) for Kafka workloads
Amazon EventBridge for event-driven architecture
explain: Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka) provisions and manages Apache Kafka broker clusters, handling ZooKeeper coordination, patching, and multi-AZ availability. It's compatible with existing Kafka client applications and tools. Amazon Kinesis is AWS's native streaming service but uses a different API.

q: A company wants to build a RESTful API for their mobile app with authentication, throttling, and caching. Which AWS service manages this?
options: AWS Lambda URL for direct function invocation via HTTP
Elastic Load Balancer for HTTP request routing
Amazon API Gateway for RESTful and WebSocket API management
AWS AppSync for GraphQL API management
explain: Amazon API Gateway creates, publishes, and manages APIs at scale — providing authentication (Cognito user pools, Lambda authorizers), rate limiting/throttling, request/response transformation, SDK generation, and caching. It integrates natively with Lambda for serverless backends and supports REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs.

q: A healthcare organization must encrypt patient data at rest. What encryption method protects stored data?
options: SSL/TLS — protecting web browser connections
Encryption at rest — protecting stored data with cryptographic keys
Network encryption — protecting communication between data centers
Encryption in transit — protecting data while moving over networks
explain: Encryption at rest protects stored data by encrypting it before writing to disk using AES-256 with keys managed by AWS KMS. Even if physical storage media were stolen or accessed without authorization, the data would be unreadable without the encryption key. Encryption in transit protects data as it moves over networks.

q: Which AWS compute service is best for running scheduled batch jobs that process large datasets overnight?
options: Amazon ECS for containerized application workloads
Amazon EC2 with scheduled Auto Scaling policies
AWS Lambda for event-driven serverless execution
AWS Batch for fully managed batch computing workloads
explain: AWS Batch dynamically provisions optimal compute resources (On-Demand, Spot, Fargate) based on job requirements and handles job scheduling, queue prioritization, and dependency management. It scales from zero to hundreds of thousands of jobs without pre-provisioning capacity, unlike running EC2 24/7 for periodic batch work.

q: A company wants to provide a shared file system accessible by multiple EC2 instances simultaneously across Availability Zones. Which service provides this?
options: Amazon EFS (Elastic File System) for shared NFS file storage across multiple AZs
Amazon S3 for object storage accessible from EC2 via API
Amazon FSx for Windows File Server for SMB protocol access
Amazon EBS volumes attached to single EC2 instances
explain: Amazon EFS (Elastic File System) is a managed NFS file system that automatically scales capacity as files are added or removed, supports simultaneous connections from thousands of EC2 instances across multiple AZs. EBS volumes attach to a single EC2 instance and cannot be shared across instances or AZs.

q: A startup evaluates cloud maturity. What is the benefit of using managed AWS services over building infrastructure from scratch?
options: Managed services cost more
Managed services are less reliable
No operational benefits
Managed services handle operations (patches, backups, scaling), letting teams focus on applications
explain: Managed AWS services (RDS, DynamoDB, Cognito, SQS) handle undifferentiated heavy lifting: patching, backups, scaling, high availability, and monitoring. Teams using managed services focus engineering time on application logic and business features rather than infrastructure operations.

q: Which AWS service provides machine learning-powered search capabilities for websites and applications?
options: Amazon CloudSearch for managed search service
Amazon Elasticsearch Service for text search workloads
Amazon Comprehend for NLP text analysis
Amazon Kendra for intelligent ML-powered enterprise search
explain: Amazon Kendra uses ML to provide intelligent enterprise search with natural language understanding — returning specific answers from documents rather than keyword-matched documents. CloudSearch and OpenSearch are traditional keyword-based search services that require query optimization and schema design.

q: A company needs to analyze their existing workloads to identify which EC2 instances to right-size before migrating to AWS. Which service provides this?
options: AWS Compute Optimizer recommending optimal EC2 instance types
AWS Migration Hub for tracking migration progress
AWS Pricing Calculator estimating costs before migration
AWS Application Discovery Service collecting on-premises server data
explain: AWS Compute Optimizer uses ML to analyze historical CloudWatch utilization metrics and recommends optimal EC2 instance types, Auto Scaling group sizes, EBS volume configurations, and Lambda memory settings. AWS Application Discovery Service is for migration planning, not optimization of existing workloads.

q: A network administrator creates a Security Group allowing HTTP and HTTPS traffic. What does this provide?
options: It encrypts all traffic automatically
It replaces the need for encryption
It eliminates the need for network infrastructure
It acts as a stateful firewall controlling traffic at the instance level
explain: Security Groups are stateful firewalls that track connection state — return traffic for allowed inbound connections is automatically permitted without explicit outbound rules. They apply at the instance network interface level and support only allow rules; denied traffic is implicitly blocked.

q: A company transitioning to cloud asks: 'What replaces our on-premises data center?' Which AWS service is the foundation?
options: CloudFront for all services
Lambda for all compute
S3 for all infrastructure
Amazon EC2 — virtual machines providing the compute foundation for cloud infrastructure
explain: Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) provides virtual machine instances that replicate the compute capabilities of traditional servers, but with on-demand provisioning, pay-per-second billing, and Auto Scaling. Unlike physical servers, EC2 instances can be launched in minutes and scaled to thousands of instances within an hour.

q: Which service provides automated deployment pipelines for continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) on AWS?
options: AWS CloudFormation for infrastructure deployment automation
AWS Systems Manager for operational automation
AWS CodePipeline for orchestrating CI/CD workflows
AWS Elastic Beanstalk for application deployment management
explain: AWS CodePipeline orchestrates CI/CD workflows by connecting source repositories (CodeCommit, GitHub), build systems (CodeBuild), and deployment targets (CodeDeploy, Elastic Beanstalk, ECS, Lambda) into automated release pipelines. CloudFormation automates infrastructure deployment but is not a CI/CD pipeline orchestrator.

q: A company implements the principle of least privilege in IAM. Why is this security best practice critical?
options: It eliminates the need for firewalls
It minimizes damage if credentials are compromised by limiting what the compromised account can do
It has no security benefit
It simplifies administration
explain: The principle of least privilege minimizes the blast radius of a security compromise: if an attacker gains a user's credentials, they can only access the resources that user needs, not the entire AWS account. This is especially critical for IAM users with programmatic access whose keys might be accidentally exposed.

q: A developer needs user authentication for a mobile app without building a login system. Which AWS service provides this?
options: AWS Shield for DDoS protection
AWS Artifact for compliance reports
AWS Cognito for user authentication and identity management
AWS KMS for encryption key management
explain: Amazon Cognito provides user pools (user directories with sign-up, sign-in, MFA, social login) and identity pools (federated identities for AWS resource access). Developers integrate Cognito SDKs without building authentication infrastructure from scratch, and Cognito scales to millions of users.

q: A company wants to quickly set up a virtual desktop for remote employees without managing physical desktops or VDI infrastructure. Which AWS service helps?
options: AWS Client VPN for secure remote network access
Amazon WorkSpaces for managed virtual desktop infrastructure
Amazon EC2 with Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP)
Amazon AppStream 2.0 for streaming individual applications
explain: Amazon WorkSpaces provides managed cloud-based Windows or Linux virtual desktops accessible from any device via the WorkSpaces client. AWS manages the hypervisor, OS patching, and backup — unlike deploying EC2 with RDP where customers manage the Windows instance. AppStream 2.0 streams individual applications, not full desktops.

q: Which AWS service enables running SQL queries directly on data stored in S3 without loading it into a database?
options: Amazon Redshift Spectrum for S3 data querying
AWS Glue ETL for transforming S3 data before querying
Amazon Athena for serverless SQL queries directly on S3 data
Amazon RDS with S3 integration for external tables
explain: Amazon Athena is a serverless interactive query service that runs standard SQL directly on data in Amazon S3 without loading it into a database or managing query infrastructure. You pay $5 per TB of data scanned, with no minimum fees. Redshift Spectrum also queries S3 but requires an existing Redshift cluster.

q: A compliance officer wants visibility into AWS resource changes. Which service should they enable?
options: AWS KMS — managing encryption
AWS GuardDuty — detecting malicious activity
AWS Config — tracking resource configuration changes
AWS Shield — blocking DDoS attacks
explain: AWS Config continuously records resource configurations and tracks all changes over time, maintaining a configuration history with a timeline view. It evaluates resources against Config Rules (managed or custom Lambda functions) and flags non-compliant resources — enabling auditors to see exactly what changed and when.

q: An organization needs to detect anomalous account activity and potential intrusions. Which service is designed for this?
options: AWS KMS for encryption management
AWS WAF for application-layer protection
AWS GuardDuty for threat detection using machine learning
AWS Shield for DDoS protection
explain: Amazon GuardDuty uses ML and threat intelligence to analyze CloudTrail logs, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs, detecting threats like compromised credentials, unusual API activity, and cryptocurrency mining. AWS WAF filters web application traffic, not account-wide anomalous behavior.

q: Which AWS service provides a globally distributed in-memory cache to reduce latency for DynamoDB tables?
options: Amazon DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) providing microsecond read performance
Amazon CloudFront caching DynamoDB query results
Amazon ElastiCache Redis in front of DynamoDB
AWS Global Accelerator routing DynamoDB requests globally
explain: Amazon DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) is a fully managed, in-memory cache specifically for DynamoDB that delivers microsecond read response times (vs single-digit millisecond for DynamoDB). It's API-compatible with DynamoDB, requiring minimal code changes, and handles cache invalidation automatically.

q: Which AWS pricing model has no upfront costs and charges by the hour or second with no long-term commitments?
options: Spot Instances with variable pricing based on capacity
On-Demand pricing with pay-as-you-go billing
Reserved Instances with upfront payment commitment
Dedicated Hosts with fixed monthly reservation fees
explain: On-Demand pricing charges for EC2 by the second (Linux) or hour (Windows) with no upfront costs or long-term commitments. It's ideal for unpredictable workloads, development environments, and short-term experiments. Reserved Instances and Savings Plans offer lower unit rates but require usage commitments.

q: A company has multiple AWS accounts. How can they consolidate billing and receive volume discounts across all accounts?
options: Enable AWS Cost Explorer across all accounts for unified billing
Use AWS Organizations with consolidated billing to combine usage across all accounts
Create a single AWS account and share access across teams
Contact AWS Sales to negotiate multi-account pricing
explain: AWS Organizations consolidated billing combines usage from all linked accounts into a single monthly invoice from the management account, simplifying payment and accounting. Combined usage may reach higher tiers in volume-tiered pricing (like S3 storage), providing discounts unavailable to individual accounts.

q: A company needs to control encryption keys used for S3, RDS, and EBS. Which service should they use?
options: AWS Secrets Manager for managing database passwords
AWS CloudHSM for on-premises encryption
AWS KMS (Key Management Service) for creating and controlling encryption keys
IAM policies for managing keys
explain: AWS KMS (Key Management Service) creates and manages Customer Managed Keys (CMKs) used to encrypt data across AWS services including S3, RDS, EBS, DynamoDB, and others. KMS provides centralized key creation, rotation, access control via key policies, and full CloudTrail audit logging of every key operation.

q: Which AWS service allows you to set custom cost and usage budgets with alerts when spending exceeds thresholds?
options: AWS Cost Explorer visualizing historical spending
AWS Cost and Usage Report for detailed billing data
AWS Budgets for proactive cost and usage alerts
AWS Pricing Calculator for estimating future costs
explain: AWS Budgets sends proactive alerts (via email or SNS) when actual or forecasted costs and usage exceed defined thresholds. Unlike CloudWatch billing alarms (which only trigger on actual charges), Budgets can alert based on forecasted spend, giving you time to take action before charges are finalized.

q: A compliance auditor requests logs of all API calls to AWS. Which service provides this audit trail?
options: AWS CloudTrail for logging API activity
AWS Config for resource configuration changes
AWS VPC Flow Logs for network traffic
AWS CloudWatch for monitoring metrics
explain: AWS CloudTrail logs every AWS API call with complete details: the IAM identity that made the call, the source IP, the timestamp, the API action performed, and the response code. This immutable audit trail is essential for security forensics, compliance audits, and detecting unauthorized account activity.

q: A company notices a large unexpected AWS bill. Which service provides a detailed breakdown of costs by service, region, and tag?
options: AWS Cost Explorer for interactive cost visualization and analysis
AWS Budgets for budget threshold management
AWS Trusted Advisor for cost optimization recommendations
AWS Cost and Usage Report for granular hourly billing data
explain: AWS Cost Explorer provides interactive visualizations of historical AWS spending with filtering by service, region, account, usage type, and cost allocation tags. It identifies spending trends, anomalies, and forecasts future costs — ideal for diagnosing unexpected bill increases by drilling into specific cost drivers.

q: Which pricing model offers flexibility to change instance types within a family while still receiving discounts compared to On-Demand?
options: Standard Reserved Instances locked to a specific instance type
Convertible Reserved Instances allowing instance type changes within commitment
Compute Savings Plans for maximum flexibility across instance families
Spot Instances with dynamic pricing based on capacity
explain: Convertible Reserved Instances allow you to exchange your RI for a different instance type, operating system, or tenancy during the term as long as the new RI has equal or greater value. They provide 66% savings versus On-Demand. Standard RIs offer higher discounts (72%) but cannot be exchanged for different attributes.
