q: A security team deploys resources in a VPC and uses Security Groups and NACLs. What layered security is this achieving?
options: Redundant and unnecessary security measures
Defense in depth — multiple security layers (network isolation + instance-level + subnet-level firewalls)
Only one of these is actually needed
No security benefit from multiple layers
explain: Defense in depth applies multiple security controls at different layers: VPC provides network isolation, Security Groups provide stateful instance-level firewall rules, and NACLs provide stateless subnet-level rules with explicit allow/deny. Each layer catches threats that might bypass another, reducing overall risk.

q: A company needs to enforce security compliance rules automatically. Which service helps enforce baselines?
options: AWS Secrets Manager for managing passwords
AWS CloudTrail for logging API calls
AWS Trusted Advisor for cost optimization only
AWS Config for tracking configurations and enforcing compliance rules
explain: AWS Config evaluates resource configurations against rules — managed rules (pre-built by AWS) or custom rules (Lambda functions) — and marks non-compliant resources. Remediation actions can automatically correct non-compliant resources. CloudTrail logs who made changes; Config records what the configuration was before and after.

q: A startup wants to reduce EC2 costs by committing to a consistent compute spend for 1 year. Which pricing option provides broad flexibility across instance types?
options: EC2 Reserved Instances for specific instance type discounts
Dedicated Hosts for compliance and licensing optimization
EC2 Spot Instances for variable workload cost reduction
Compute Savings Plans for flexible savings across any EC2 instance type
explain: Compute Savings Plans commit to a consistent hourly compute spend (e.g., $10/hr) for 1 or 3 years, automatically applying discounts to any EC2 instance type, family, size, region, OS, or tenancy — plus Lambda and Fargate. This is more flexible than Reserved Instances, which lock you into specific instance attributes.

q: Which AWS service provides a detailed, granular billing report with hourly or daily line items for every AWS service usage?
options: AWS Cost Explorer for interactive spending visualization
AWS Pricing Calculator for future cost estimation
AWS Budgets for threshold-based cost monitoring
AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR) for comprehensive billing data
explain: AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR) provides the most granular billing data available: hourly or daily line items for every AWS service, resource ID, pricing details, and cost allocation tag values. It's delivered to S3 as CSV or Parquet and is designed for custom analysis with Athena, Redshift, or BI tools.

q: A company wants AWS to automatically alert them if their bill is forecasted to exceed $1,000 this month before they actually overspend. Which tool enables this?
options: AWS Pricing Calculator estimating costs upfront
AWS Budgets with forecasted cost alerts
AWS Trusted Advisor reviewing cost optimization opportunities
AWS Cost Explorer showing historical trends
explain: AWS Budgets can send alerts when forecasted costs are projected to exceed your budget threshold, giving you time to take action before charges are incurred. This is more proactive than Cost Explorer (shows historical data) or CloudWatch billing alarms (only trigger on actual charges, not forecasted ones).

q: What is the AWS Support plan that provides production system impaired response within 4 hours?
options: Enterprise Support — 15-minute critical system down response
Basic Support — free tier with no production SLAs
Business Support — 24/7 phone/chat with 4-hour production impaired response
Developer Support — business hours email support only
explain: AWS Business Support provides 24/7 phone, chat, and email access to Cloud Support Engineers with response SLAs of: less than 1 hour for production system down, less than 4 hours for production system impaired, and less than 12 hours for general guidance cases. Developer Support only provides business hours email.

q: What AWS service provides recommendations to reduce EC2 costs by identifying idle and underutilized instances?
options: AWS Compute Optimizer right-sizing recommendations
AWS Cost Explorer Reserved Instance recommendations
AWS Config checking resource configuration compliance
AWS Trusted Advisor identifying cost optimization opportunities
explain: AWS Trusted Advisor checks for idle EC2 instances, unattached EBS volumes, underutilized RDS instances, and low-utilization load balancers, recommending termination or downsizing. AWS Compute Optimizer provides ML-based right-sizing recommendations specifically for EC2 instance families, complementing Trusted Advisor.

q: A company purchases Reserved Instances for EC2 but their usage pattern changes and the RIs no longer align with their workload. What can they do?
options: Convert Reserved Instances to On-Demand automatically
Contact AWS to cancel the Reserved Instances immediately
Sell unused Reserved Instances on the AWS Reserved Instance Marketplace
Transfer Reserved Instances between AWS accounts for free
explain: The AWS Reserved Instance Marketplace allows customers to list and sell unused Standard Reserved Instances to other AWS customers before the term expires. This provides an exit option when workloads change and RIs no longer match usage patterns. Convertible RIs cannot be sold on the Marketplace.

q: Which AWS service enables you to view and manage all of your AWS costs from a single, integrated view even across multiple payer accounts?
options: AWS Organizations for multi-account consolidated billing view
AWS Cost Management console providing unified cost visibility
AWS Cost and Usage Report for detailed billing data export
AWS Cost Explorer for a single account cost analysis
explain: AWS Cost Management console includes Cost Explorer, Budgets, Cost and Usage Reports, and Savings Plans — all accessible from a unified interface that works across all linked accounts in AWS Organizations. This enables cross-account cost analysis, budget management, and optimization recommendations from a single view.

q: A company wants to enable encryption for data in transit (moving between EC2 and database). Which technology provides this?
options: VPC isolation only
SSL/TLS — encrypts data while moving over networks
Encryption at rest only
No encryption for data in transit
explain: SSL/TLS encrypts data in transit between the application and the RDS endpoint, protecting against network eavesdropping. Combined with encryption at rest (KMS-encrypted EBS volumes), this protects data in all states. VPC isolation keeps traffic off the public internet but does not encrypt the traffic within the connection.

q: A company wants to track how much each team is spending on AWS. What is the best practice for cost attribution?
options: Ask teams to self-report their AWS resource usage monthly
Create separate AWS accounts for each team
Use consistent cost allocation tags (e.g., team=engineering) on all resources and activate them in billing
Review monthly bills manually and estimate team costs
explain: Cost allocation tags (e.g., Team=Engineering, Project=Analytics, Environment=Production) are key-value pairs applied to AWS resources. After activating them in Billing Preferences, they appear in Cost Explorer and the Cost and Usage Report, enabling granular cost breakdowns by team, project, or environment.

q: A company runs a web application on EC2 and experiences sudden traffic spike. How does Auto Scaling respond?
options: No solution exists for traffic spikes
Auto Scaling automatically launches additional EC2 instances to handle the load
Servers crash from overload
Manual intervention is required
explain: EC2 Auto Scaling Groups monitor CloudWatch metrics (CPU utilization, custom metrics) and automatically launch additional EC2 instances when load increases, then terminate instances when load decreases. This maintains application performance during spikes without requiring manual intervention or pre-provisioned excess capacity.

q: Which AWS tool can help identify which EC2 Reserved Instances are underutilized and recommend purchasing options?
options: AWS Cost Explorer with RI utilization and coverage reports
AWS Budgets tracking RI coverage percentage
AWS Trusted Advisor checking RI utilization rates
AWS Pricing Calculator for evaluating purchase options
explain: AWS Cost Explorer provides Reserved Instance utilization reports (what percentage of purchased RI capacity was actually used) and coverage reports (what percentage of total EC2 usage was covered by RIs). It also generates RI purchase recommendations based on your historical On-Demand usage patterns.

q: A company is choosing between AWS and building their own data center. Which total cost of ownership (TCO) factors favor AWS?
options: AWS pricing is transparent while on-premises has no hidden costs
AWS always costs less than on-premises for every workload
On-premises is always cheaper for steady-state workloads with predictable demand
AWS eliminates server hardware purchase, data center facility costs, power/cooling, and staff for infrastructure management
explain: AWS TCO advantages include: no capital expenditure on server hardware, no data center facility costs (power, cooling, space, physical security), no hardware lifecycle management, reduced staffing for infrastructure operations, and free AWS services within the Free Tier. The AWS Pricing Calculator quantifies these savings.

q: AWS Trusted Advisor flags: 'security group allows unrestricted SSH access (0.0.0.0/0).' What risk does this represent?
options: Risk only applies to production environments
High risk: anyone on the internet can attempt to connect via SSH
No risk; SSH is encrypted so it's always safe
Risk is eliminated if you use strong passwords
explain: Allowing SSH (port 22) from 0.0.0.0/0 exposes EC2 instances to brute force attacks, automated bot scanning, and exploitation of any known SSH vulnerabilities from any internet host. Best practice: restrict SSH to specific IP ranges, use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for browser-based shell access with no open ports.

q: Which AWS Support plan includes Infrastructure Event Management for major launches and migrations at no additional cost?
options: Business Support included in standard pricing
Enterprise On-Ramp for production workloads
Enterprise Support providing proactive engagement including IEM
Developer Support for growing companies
explain: Enterprise Support includes Infrastructure Event Management (IEM), where AWS Solutions Architects provide hands-on planning and operational guidance for major events such as product launches, database migrations, and high-traffic events. Business Support does not include IEM as a standard benefit.

q: What are the 6 R's of cloud migration strategies? Which strategy involves rehosting an application without changes?
options: Replatform — making minor optimizations without changing core architecture
Refactor — redesigning the application to be cloud-native
Retire — decommissioning applications that are no longer needed
Rehost (lift-and-shift) — moving the application to cloud with no changes
explain: Rehosting (lift-and-shift) migrates applications to AWS exactly as they are with no code changes — typically using AWS Application Migration Service to replicate servers to EC2. It is the fastest path to the cloud. Refactoring rebuilds applications as cloud-native; Replatforming makes minor optimizations.

q: A company wants to replace their self-managed CRM with Salesforce on AWS. Which migration strategy is this?
options: Rehost — moving existing CRM to EC2 without changes
Replatform — upgrading the database engine during migration
Repurchase — dropping the current app and switching to a different SaaS product
Refactor — rebuilding the CRM as cloud-native microservices
explain: Repurchase (also called 'drop-and-shop') abandons the current application entirely and subscribes to a SaaS replacement: Salesforce for CRM, Workday for HR, ServiceNow for ITSM. No data migration of the old application is needed — only data export and import to the new SaaS product.

q: A startup needs compute capacity with flexibility to scale. Which AWS service fits this?
options: Amazon RDS for databases only
Amazon S3 for storage only
Amazon EC2 for virtual machine instances with auto scaling
AWS Lambda for serverless functions
explain: Amazon EC2 provides resizable virtual machines with hundreds of instance types optimized for different workloads (compute, memory, storage, GPU). Auto Scaling Groups automatically add or remove instances based on demand, enabling cost-efficient scaling from one instance to thousands without pre-purchasing capacity.

q: A team runs data analytics requiring high CPU and memory. Which EC2 instance family is optimized for this?
options: I-family for storage-optimized workloads
C-family for compute-optimized workloads with high CPU
R-family for memory-optimized workloads
T-family for burstable workloads
explain: Amazon EC2 R-family instances are memory-optimized with high RAM-to-CPU ratios, ideal for in-memory analytics, large dataset processing, and memory-intensive applications. C-family instances are compute-optimized for high CPU workloads. For data analytics requiring both high CPU and memory, R-family provides the best balance.

q: A company has a steady, long-term application. What EC2 pricing model minimizes cost?
options: Spot Instances for 70-90% discount but can be interrupted
Dedicated Hosts for specific licensing
Reserved Instances (1 or 3-year commitment) for 30-70% discounts
On-Demand pricing for maximum flexibility
explain: Reserved Instances with 1 or 3-year commitment offer 30-72% discount versus On-Demand pricing. All-upfront payment provides the maximum discount. For a steady-state database running 24/7 for 3 years, the predictable usage pattern makes RIs optimal — Spot Instances risk interruption for database workloads.

q: Which disaster recovery strategy offers the fastest recovery time but highest cost, by keeping a fully operational duplicate environment always running?
options: Multi-Site Active/Active — full production capacity in multiple regions simultaneously
Pilot Light — minimal critical systems always running, scale up when needed
Warm Standby — scaled-down but fully functional version always running
Backup and Restore — cheapest but slowest, restore from backup when needed
explain: Multi-Site Active/Active runs full production infrastructure simultaneously in multiple AWS regions, with traffic distributed across all regions. Failover is instant (near-zero RTO and RPO) but costs approximately 2x since full infrastructure runs continuously in multiple regions. It's the most expensive but most resilient DR strategy.

q: A company wants the cheapest disaster recovery strategy and can tolerate several hours of downtime. Which approach should they use?
options: Pilot Light with only core database systems running
Multi-Site Active/Active for zero downtime
Backup and Restore — back up data to S3 and restore EC2 instances when needed
Warm Standby with a scaled-down live environment
explain: Backup and Restore is the lowest-cost disaster recovery strategy: back up data to S3 with automated backups, create AMI snapshots of EC2 instances. During a disaster, provision new resources from these backups. RTO is several hours but the cost is near zero during normal operations when no standby infrastructure runs.

q: Which AWS service helps track the cost of AWS resources by assigning metadata labels to resources for cost allocation?
options: AWS Cost Explorer analyzing spending patterns
AWS Trusted Advisor recommending cost optimizations
Cost allocation tags identifying and tracking resource costs by project or team
AWS Budgets setting spending alerts
explain: Cost allocation tags are metadata (key-value pairs) attached to AWS resources that appear in billing data after being activated in Billing Preferences. In Cost Explorer, tags become filter dimensions — e.g., filtering by Project=Analytics shows exactly what that project costs across all services and regions.

q: A company wants to serve static website assets globally with low latency. Which AWS service is designed for this?
options: Amazon CloudFront — a CDN caching content at edge locations worldwide
Amazon RDS for database-backed content
AWS Lambda for serverless websites
EC2 instances in multiple regions
explain: Amazon CloudFront caches content at 400+ Points of Presence globally, serving cached assets from the location closest to the end user rather than requiring requests to travel back to the origin server. This reduces latency for global users and reduces origin server bandwidth and load.

q: Which AWS Support plan is the minimum required to get 24/7 phone and chat access to Cloud Support Engineers?
options: Business Support — 24/7 phone and chat with Cloud Support Engineers
Enterprise Support — dedicated TAM plus 24/7 support
Developer Support — business hours email support only
Basic Support — free but limited to documentation and forums
explain: AWS Business Support (minimum $100/month or percentage of usage) is the minimum plan providing 24/7 phone and chat access to AWS Cloud Support Engineers. Developer Support provides only business hours email. Enterprise Support adds a dedicated Technical Account Manager (TAM) and Infrastructure Event Management.

q: What response time SLA does Business Support provide for production system down cases?
options: 24 hours — next business day response
12 hours — same day business hours response
4 hours — urgent response for impaired production systems
1 hour — for production system down cases
explain: AWS Business Support guarantees: less than 1 hour response for production system DOWN, less than 4 hours for production system IMPAIRED, and less than 12 hours for general guidance. Enterprise Support improves the critical SLA to less than 15 minutes for business-critical system down cases.

q: Which AWS tool provides automated recommendations across cost, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits?
options: AWS Config checking configuration compliance against rules
Amazon CloudWatch monitoring metrics and setting alarms
AWS Well-Architected Tool for structured architecture review
AWS Trusted Advisor providing real-time best practice recommendations
explain: AWS Trusted Advisor provides automated best practice checks across five categories: Cost Optimization (idle resources, RI recommendations), Security (open security groups, IAM), Fault Tolerance (Multi-AZ, backups), Performance (provisioned throughput), and Service Limits. Business and Enterprise plans unlock all checks.

q: An application needs to store objects like files, images, and videos. Which AWS service is optimized for this?
options: Amazon EC2 for compute and storage
Amazon DynamoDB for NoSQL tables
Amazon S3 for object storage with unlimited scalability
Amazon RDS for structured databases
explain: Amazon S3 is an object storage service designed for any type of data (files, images, videos, backups, logs) with unlimited scalability, 99.999999999% durability, and pay-per-GB pricing. EBS provides block storage for EC2 instances; RDS stores structured relational data — neither is designed for general object storage.

q: A company archives data rarely accessed. Which S3 storage class optimizes for long-term archival?
options: S3 Intelligent-Tiering for automatic optimization
S3 Standard for frequently accessed data
S3 One Zone-IA for single AZ infrequent access
S3 Glacier or S3 Glacier Deep Archive for archival with very low cost
explain: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval and S3 Glacier Deep Archive provide the lowest storage costs ($0.004/GB and $0.00099/GB respectively) for archival data rarely accessed. Both have 90-day minimum storage duration. Standard S3 tiers cost significantly more for data that doesn't need frequent access.

q: Which cloud deployment model uses dedicated hardware for a single organization, hosted and managed by a third-party provider?
options: Hybrid cloud — combining on-premises with public cloud
Hosted private cloud — dedicated hardware managed by a third party
On-premises private cloud — owned and operated by the organization
Public cloud — multi-tenant shared AWS infrastructure
explain: Hosted private cloud provides dedicated (single-tenant) hardware operated by a third-party provider — like AWS Outposts, which runs AWS infrastructure on-premises at the customer's facility but is managed by AWS. This combines dedicated hardware (compliance-friendly) with managed operations.

q: A developer uses Amazon S3 and wants to prevent accidental deletion of critical objects. Which S3 feature provides this?
options: Only S3 Glacier provides protection
S3 has no deletion protection
Deletion is always immediate
S3 Object Lock — protects objects from deletion/overwrite for compliance (WORM)
explain: S3 Object Lock enables Write Once Read Many (WORM) protection, preventing objects from being deleted or overwritten for a specified retention period — even by root account users. It's essential for compliance requirements (SEC 17a-4, FINRA, CFTC) that mandate immutable audit records.

q: Which pricing model allows customers to commit to a consistent compute usage amount in exchange for up to 72% discount vs On-Demand?
options: Dedicated Hosts — pay for a physical server dedicated to your use
Spot Instances — bid for unused EC2 capacity at up to 90% discount
On-Demand — pay for compute by the hour with no commitment
Reserved Instances or Savings Plans — commit to usage for 1 or 3 years for discount
explain: Reserved Instances and Savings Plans both offer up to 72% discount versus On-Demand pricing in exchange for 1 or 3-year usage commitments. Spot Instances offer up to 90% discount but can be interrupted with 2-minute notice — unsuitable for long-term steady-state workloads with defined business SLAs.

q: A company is migrating and wants AWS to help identify which applications to move first. Which service provides migration assessment and planning?
options: AWS Migration Hub for tracking migrations across services
AWS Application Discovery Service gathering on-premises data for migration planning
AWS Database Migration Service for moving databases
AWS Schema Conversion Tool for database migration
explain: AWS Application Discovery Service collects server configuration, performance, and network dependency data from on-premises environments to help prioritize which applications to migrate and identify dependencies. AWS Migration Hub aggregates migration progress but relies on Application Discovery for assessment data.

q: A company needs a managed relational database without managing software or hardware. Which service fits?
options: Amazon RDS — managed relational database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, Aurora)
Amazon DynamoDB for NoSQL only
Amazon S3 for structured data
EC2 instances with self-managed MySQL
explain: Amazon RDS manages MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Aurora database engines with automated patching, backups, Multi-AZ failover, and storage management. Installing MySQL on EC2 gives more control but requires the customer to handle all patching, backup configuration, and high availability setup.

q: A startup evaluates Aurora versus traditional RDS. What is Aurora's primary advantage?
options: Up to 5x faster than RDS with automatic scaling and high availability
Cheaper than RDS
Suitable only for NoSQL workloads
Requires manual database administration
explain: Amazon Aurora is a cloud-native relational database engine that achieves up to 5x the throughput of standard MySQL and 3x PostgreSQL by using a distributed, SSD-backed storage layer that replicates data across six nodes in three AZs. Aurora is fully compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL wire protocols.

q: Which Well-Architected pillar focuses on the ability of a workload to perform its intended function correctly and consistently, and recover from failures?
options: Performance Efficiency — using resources efficiently
Operational Excellence — running and improving operations
Reliability — recovering from failures and meeting demand
Security — protecting information and systems
explain: The Reliability pillar covers: automatic recovery from infrastructure failures, testing recovery procedures, horizontal scaling for demand changes, and managing change through automation. AWS services supporting Reliability include Route 53 health checks, RDS Multi-AZ, and EC2 Auto Scaling Groups.

q: A company runs batch processing jobs triggered on-demand. Which service optimizes cost?
options: Amazon ECS for container orchestration
AWS Lambda — serverless compute triggered on-demand, paying per invocation
AWS Batch only
Running EC2 instances 24/7
explain: AWS Lambda is ideal for event-driven batch processing: triggered by S3 events, SQS messages, or scheduled EventBridge rules; scales automatically from zero to 1,000+ concurrent executions; and charges only for the duration of execution (per 1ms) rather than for continuous running EC2 instances.

q: Which AWS service helps customers move petabyte-scale data to AWS when internet transfer would take too long?
options: AWS DataSync for online data transfer via internet or Direct Connect
AWS Snowball Edge — physical device that ships to your data center for offline data transfer
AWS Transfer Family for SFTP-based data transfer
Amazon Kinesis for streaming real-time data to AWS
explain: The AWS Snow family (Snowball, Snowball Edge, Snowmobile) provides physical devices for offline data transfer when network bandwidth is insufficient. For petabyte-scale transfers, Snowball Edge stores 80TB per device — shipping data is faster and cheaper than waiting weeks or months for internet transfer.

q: What does the 'Sustainability' pillar (6th pillar) of the AWS Well-Architected Framework focus on?
options: Minimizing environmental impact by reducing energy consumption and carbon footprint
Sustainable pricing through long-term Reserved Instance commitments
Ensuring workloads run sustainably without interruption
Long-term business sustainability and profitability in the cloud
explain: The Sustainability pillar (added in 2021 as the sixth pillar) focuses on minimizing environmental impact: right-sizing resources to reduce waste, using ARM-based Graviton processors for better performance-per-watt, optimizing code efficiency, and choosing AWS regions powered by renewable energy.

q: A team discusses 'serverless architecture.' What does 'serverless' mean for AWS Lambda?
options: All AWS services are serverless
No servers are involved
Code runs without the internet
AWS manages servers: developers write functions; AWS handles provisioning, scaling, patching
explain: Serverless doesn't mean no servers exist — AWS servers run the code, but developers never see, configure, or manage them. AWS Lambda abstracts all server management: provisioning, OS patching, scaling, and availability. Developers write functions and define triggers; AWS handles all infrastructure.

q: Which AWS program lets startups get AWS credits, training, and support to build their businesses on AWS?
options: AWS Marketplace for purchasing startup software solutions
AWS Free Tier for new accounts experimenting with services
AWS Migration Acceleration Program for enterprise migrations
AWS Activate providing credits, training, and support specifically for startups
explain: AWS Activate provides startups with AWS credits (up to $100,000+ depending on tier), technical training, business support resources, and access to AWS Partner Network connections through accelerators, incubators, and VC firms. AWS Free Tier is available to all new accounts regardless of startup status.

q: A company needs a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). What are the primary VPC components?
options: Only EC2 instances
Subnets (public/private), Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, Route Tables, Security Groups, NACLs
VPCs don't provide network isolation
Just a security group
explain: An Amazon VPC consists of subnets (public and private CIDR blocks), an Internet Gateway (for public subnet internet access), a NAT Gateway (for private subnet outbound access), Route Tables (directing traffic), Security Groups (instance-level stateful firewall), and Network ACLs (subnet-level stateless firewall).

q: A financial company needs its AWS resources to only be accessible over a private dedicated network connection, not the public internet. Which service provides this?
options: VPC Peering connecting two VPCs privately
VPN Gateway using encrypted tunnels over the internet
AWS Direct Connect — dedicated private fiber connection from data center to AWS
AWS PrivateLink for private connectivity to AWS services
explain: AWS Direct Connect establishes a dedicated private fiber connection from the customer's data center to AWS over a 1Gbps or 10Gbps circuit through an AWS Direct Connect Location. This bypasses the public internet, providing consistent performance, lower latency, and reduced data transfer pricing.

q: What is the purpose of the AWS Service Health Dashboard vs AWS Personal Health Dashboard?
options: Service Health Dashboard is for enterprise customers; Personal Health Dashboard is for individual developers
Service Health Dashboard shows all AWS service status globally; Personal Health Dashboard shows issues affecting YOUR specific resources
Personal Health Dashboard costs extra; Service Health Dashboard is free
They are identical dashboards showing the same information
explain: The AWS Service Health Dashboard (status.aws.amazon.com) shows global AWS service status and historical availability for all services in all regions. AWS Personal Health Dashboard (AWS Health) shows events specifically affecting YOUR resources and sends proactive notifications for planned maintenance on your specific services.

q: Private database instances need internet access for updates. How is this provided securely?
options: Opening all ports to the internet
Private subnets cannot access the internet
Using a NAT Gateway in the public subnet for outbound-only access
Private databases don't need updates
explain: A NAT (Network Address Translation) Gateway in a public subnet allows instances in private subnets to initiate outbound connections to the internet (for software updates, patches, API calls) while blocking all inbound connections from the internet — maintaining the security of private subnet resources.

q: A company has multiple AWS accounts and needs a centralized place to manage all security findings. Which service provides this unified security view?
options: IAM Access Analyzer analyzing resource permissions
AWS Config tracking compliance across all accounts
AWS Security Hub aggregating findings from multiple security services across accounts
Amazon GuardDuty for threat detection across accounts
explain: AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and partner security tools into a centralized security dashboard with a prioritized list of findings. It runs automated compliance checks against CIS, PCI DSS, and AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standards.

q: An architect designs DNS routing for global applications. Which service routes traffic geographically?
options: Security Groups for routing
AWS Lambda for serverless routing
Amazon CloudFront only
Amazon Route 53 — AWS's DNS with geolocation routing
explain: Amazon Route 53 provides authoritative DNS with multiple routing policies: geolocation (route by user's country/continent), latency-based (route to lowest-latency region), weighted (A/B traffic splitting), failover (health check-based failover), and multivalue (return multiple healthy endpoints).

q: Which AWS service enables single sign-on for employees to access multiple AWS accounts and business applications with one set of credentials?
options: AWS Directory Service for Microsoft Active Directory integration
AWS IAM for individual user authentication
Amazon Cognito for customer-facing application authentication
AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) for centralized single sign-on across accounts and applications
explain: AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS Single Sign-On) provides centralized access management to multiple AWS accounts and SAML 2.0 applications. Employees sign in once using corporate credentials from Active Directory or any SAML 2.0 IdP and gain access to all authorized accounts and applications.

q: What is the difference between security groups and Network Access Control Lists (NACLs) in AWS?
options: Security groups are for databases; NACLs are for web servers
Security groups require more configuration; NACLs are automatically configured
NACLs are stricter and override security group settings
Security groups are stateful instance-level firewalls; NACLs are stateless subnet-level firewalls with explicit allow and deny rules
explain: Security Groups are stateful: return traffic for allowed inbound connections is automatically permitted without explicit outbound rules. NACLs are stateless: return traffic requires explicit allow rules in both directions. Security Groups apply to instances; NACLs apply to subnets. NACLs support both allow and deny rules.

q: What is AWS Shield Advanced and when would a company need it over Shield Standard?
options: Shield Advanced adds DDoS cost protection, attack visibility, response team access, and advanced attack mitigation for sophisticated DDoS attacks
Shield Standard for small companies; Shield Advanced only for enterprises
Shield Advanced provides WAF rules; Shield Standard does not
Shield Advanced is required for all production AWS workloads
explain: AWS Shield Advanced ($3,000/month with 1-year commitment) provides enhanced DDoS detection, 24/7 access to the AWS DDoS Response Team (DRT), near real-time attack dashboards in CloudWatch, and cost protection — credit for EC2 or data transfer charges incurred due to DDoS-related scaling. Shield Standard is free.

q: Which AWS service helps prevent accidental public exposure of S3 buckets and other resources by analyzing resource policies?
options: AWS Config checking S3 bucket public access settings
Amazon Macie scanning S3 for sensitive data exposure
Amazon GuardDuty detecting unauthorized S3 access
IAM Access Analyzer identifying resources accessible from outside your account
explain: IAM Access Analyzer examines resource-based policies for S3 buckets, IAM roles, KMS keys, Lambda functions, and SQS queues to identify resources accessible from outside your account. It alerts on unintended public access or cross-account sharing — Amazon Macie identifies sensitive data content within S3 objects.

q: Under the shared responsibility model, who is responsible for patching the operating system on an EC2 instance?
options: Both AWS and customer share OS patching responsibility equally
AWS — they manage all software running on their infrastructure
Neither — EC2 instances automatically patch themselves
The customer — EC2 is IaaS so customers manage the OS and above
explain: For EC2 (IaaS), customers own the OS layer and everything above: they must apply operating system patches, manage application code, rotate credentials, configure security groups, and encrypt data. AWS owns the hypervisor and physical infrastructure. This contrasts with RDS where AWS patches the database engine OS.

q: A company balances traffic across multiple web servers automatically. Which service distributes traffic?
options: AWS Elastic Load Balancing (ALB, NLB, CLB) — distributes traffic with health checks
Route 53 for DNS only
EC2 Auto Scaling alone
Security Groups for load balancing
explain: AWS Elastic Load Balancing automatically distributes incoming traffic across multiple healthy targets (EC2 instances, containers, Lambda functions) using health checks to route only to healthy targets. ALB operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS); NLB at Layer 4 (TCP/UDP); both integrate with Auto Scaling.

q: Which compliance framework is most relevant for companies storing healthcare data (PHI) in AWS?
options: SOC 2 for service organization security controls
ISO 27001 for information security management systems
HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) for protected health information
PCI DSS for payment card data protection
explain: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) governs protected health information (PHI) in the United States. AWS signs Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) with covered healthcare entities and provides HIPAA-eligible services with appropriate safeguards for storing, processing, and transmitting PHI.

q: What is 'encryption in transit' and which protocol implements it for web traffic?
options: Compressing data for faster network transmission
Encrypting data before it leaves your application server
Encrypting data as it travels across networks using TLS/SSL protocols
Encrypting data on disk using AES-256 encryption
explain: Encryption in transit (TLS 1.2/1.3) encrypts data as it travels across networks between clients, application servers, and AWS services. AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) provides free TLS/SSL certificates for CloudFront, ALB, and API Gateway. Encryption at rest protects stored data — both are needed for complete protection.

q: A company needs to ensure no one in their organization can create resources in regions outside of us-east-1 and eu-west-1. What is the best approach?
options: Create IAM policies denying all other regions for each user
Configure AWS Config rules alerting on resources in other regions
Set up CloudTrail alarms for API calls in unauthorized regions
Use AWS Organizations SCPs to deny all actions in non-approved regions
explain: AWS Organizations SCPs can include Condition keys (aws:RequestedRegion) that deny all API actions in non-approved regions. SCPs apply to all accounts in the organization and cannot be bypassed by account administrators, providing stronger region enforcement than IAM policies which account admins can modify.

q: What AWS service continuously monitors and records AWS resource configurations, enabling compliance auditing and change tracking?
options: Amazon CloudWatch monitoring metrics and system performance
AWS CloudTrail recording all API calls made in the account
AWS Config recording resource configurations and changes over time
AWS Security Hub aggregating security compliance findings
explain: AWS Config continuously records resource configuration states and changes — creating a timeline of what a resource looked like at any point in history. Config Rules evaluate resources against compliance standards and flag violations. CloudTrail records who made changes; Config records what the resulting configuration was.

q: A company stores data in RDS and wants automated failover if the primary database fails. What RDS feature enables this?
options: Manual failover is required
RDS Multi-AZ synchronously replicates data to a standby, automatically promoting the standby on failure
Only DynamoDB supports automatic failover
Data is never replicated in RDS
explain: RDS Multi-AZ maintains a synchronous standby replica in a different Availability Zone. On primary failure, AWS automatically promotes the standby — DNS failover completes in 60-120 seconds. Unlike read replicas (asynchronous, for scaling reads), Multi-AZ standby only promotes during failures.

q: Which AWS service provides managed Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) protection and is automatically applied to all AWS customers at no charge?
options: AWS Network Firewall — managed VPC firewall service
AWS WAF — web application firewall filtering layer 7 attacks
AWS Shield Standard — automatic protection against network/transport layer DDoS at no cost
Amazon GuardDuty — detecting volumetric attack patterns
explain: AWS Shield Standard is built into the AWS global network and automatically protects all AWS resources from common volumetric (Layer 3) and protocol (Layer 4) DDoS attacks at no additional cost. Every AWS customer benefits from Shield Standard without any configuration required.

q: A company wants to ensure that all EC2 instances in their AWS account always have encrypted EBS volumes. What is the most efficient way to enforce this?
options: Manually encrypt each EBS volume when launching instances
Enable default EBS encryption in the account settings to encrypt all new volumes automatically
Use AWS Config rules to alert when unencrypted volumes are found
Create an IAM policy preventing creation of unencrypted volumes
explain: Enabling EBS encryption by default at the account level automatically encrypts all newly created EBS volumes and snapshots using KMS, regardless of who creates them or which launch template is used. This is simpler and more reliable than requiring individual developers to enable encryption on each launch.

q: A company needs to alert teams when CPU usage exceeds 80%. Which AWS service provides this?
options: AWS Systems Manager for resource management
AWS Trusted Advisor for cost optimization
AWS CloudWatch — provides metrics, monitoring, and alarms
AWS Config for compliance tracking
explain: Amazon CloudWatch collects metrics from hundreds of AWS services (CPU utilization, disk I/O, network throughput) and custom application metrics. Alarms trigger SNS notifications or Auto Scaling actions when metrics breach thresholds. CloudWatch is the central observability service for operational monitoring.

q: Which AWS service provides a fully managed network firewall for filtering traffic entering and leaving VPCs?
options: AWS WAF filtering HTTP traffic at the application layer
Network ACLs filtering subnet traffic
Security groups filtering EC2 instance traffic
AWS Network Firewall providing stateful deep packet inspection for VPCs
explain: AWS Network Firewall is a managed VPC firewall providing stateful deep packet inspection (DPI), intrusion detection and prevention (IDS/IPS) using Suricata-compatible rule signatures, and URL filtering. Unlike Security Groups and NACLs, Network Firewall can inspect and block malicious traffic content, not just IP/port combinations.

q: A healthcare provider requires database high availability with automatic failover. Which service provides this?
options: No AWS service provides automatic failover
Amazon RDS with Multi-AZ deployment — automatic failover on failure
Self-managed MySQL on EC2
Amazon S3 for databases
explain: RDS Multi-AZ synchronously replicates data from the primary to a standby instance in a different AZ. On failure, RDS automatically updates the DNS endpoint to point to the standby — application reconnects without manual intervention. This typically takes 60-120 seconds and requires no application code changes.

q: A company must demonstrate to auditors that AWS meets specific compliance standards. What self-service AWS resource provides compliance documentation?
options: AWS Trusted Advisor showing security compliance best practices
AWS Security Hub showing compliance scores and findings
AWS Artifact providing on-demand AWS compliance reports, certifications, and agreements
AWS Config providing configuration compliance reports
explain: AWS Artifact is a self-service portal providing on-demand downloads of AWS compliance reports (SOC 1/2/3, PCI DSS, ISO 27001/27017/27018, HIPAA, FedRAMP) and compliance agreements (BAAs, NDAs). It enables customers to provide auditors with documentation of AWS's compliance posture directly.

q: A healthcare platform needs daily backups and point-in-time recovery. Which RDS feature enables this?
options: Manual snapshots only
RDS automated backups — continuous daily backups enabling point-in-time recovery up to 35 days
S3 backup system
No automated backups available
explain: RDS automated backups create daily full database snapshots and capture transaction logs throughout the day, enabling point-in-time recovery to any second within the retention period (1-35 days). Manual snapshots persist indefinitely after deletion but don't support point-in-time recovery between snapshots.

q: A company uses CloudFront to serve content globally. What primary benefit does it provide?
options: CloudFront caches content at edge locations globally, reducing latency and bandwidth costs
Direct S3 is faster than CloudFront
CloudFront is more expensive
CloudFront adds security
explain: CloudFront reduces latency by caching content at edge locations near users — a user in Tokyo downloading from a Frankfurt origin would experience ~200ms latency, but downloading from a Tokyo edge location drops latency to ~5ms. Origin bandwidth and server load are also reduced by serving cached responses.

q: Which AWS service enables federated access to AWS using existing corporate identities from Microsoft Active Directory?
options: AWS IAM creating native AWS user accounts
AWS IAM Identity Center for multi-account SSO
AWS Directory Service connecting AWS to Microsoft AD or running Managed AD in the cloud
Amazon Cognito for customer application identity
explain: AWS Directory Service offers three options: AWS Managed Microsoft AD (fully managed AD in the cloud), AD Connector (proxy to on-premises AD without directory sync), and Simple AD (basic LDAP-compatible directory). Employees can use existing Windows domain credentials to access AWS resources via domain join.

q: A startup doesn't have dedicated security staff. Which AWS service provides automated security recommendations based on best practices for free?
options: AWS Trusted Advisor Basic providing core security checks for free
AWS Security Hub aggregating security findings
Amazon Inspector running vulnerability assessments
Amazon GuardDuty providing ML-based threat detection
explain: AWS Trusted Advisor Basic (free for all accounts) provides security checks: S3 bucket public access, security groups with unrestricted ports, IAM usage, root MFA status, and public EBS/RDS snapshots. Business and Enterprise plans unlock additional security checks including CloudTrail logging status.

q: What are the S3 storage classes and which is most appropriate for frequently accessed data requiring millisecond access?
options: S3 Standard — high durability, availability, and performance for frequently accessed data
S3 Intelligent-Tiering — automatically moves data between tiers
S3 Standard-IA — infrequent access tier for active data
S3 Glacier — cheapest storage for frequently accessed data
explain: S3 Standard provides 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability, 99.99% availability, and millisecond access with no retrieval fees. It's designed for frequently accessed data like web application assets, active datasets, and content. S3 Standard-IA and Glacier tiers add retrieval fees, making Standard optimal for frequent access.

q: A company stores compliance data that is rarely accessed but must be retrievable within 12 hours at minimal cost. Which S3 storage class is best?
options: S3 Express One Zone — single-AZ for frequently accessed data
S3 Standard — high performance but expensive for rarely accessed data
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval — low-cost archival with retrieval times from minutes to 12 hours
S3 Standard-IA — lower storage cost with retrieval fee for infrequent access
explain: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval provides 90-day minimum storage duration and three retrieval speed options: Expedited (1-5 minutes), Standard (3-5 hours), and Bulk (5-12 hours). For compliance archives accessed rarely but retrievable within 12 hours, Glacier Flexible Retrieval offers dramatically lower costs than S3 Standard.

q: An architect designs an application requiring global low-latency content delivery. CloudFront is selected for the CDN. Why?
options: CloudFront is expensive and slow
CloudFront caches content at 400+ edge locations globally, serving users from nearby locations
CloudFront only works in the US
Direct S3 is faster than CloudFront
explain: CloudFront caches content at 400+ Points of Presence (edge locations and regional edge caches) globally. Users receive content from the nearest location rather than the origin server, reducing round-trip latency and bandwidth costs. For applications with global users, CloudFront is essential for consistent performance.

q: A startup needs to monitor applications for errors and performance. Which AWS service provides detailed insights?
options: AWS X-Ray — traces requests end-to-end, revealing bottlenecks and errors
AWS CloudTrail for API logging only
Route 53 for DNS only
CloudFront for CDN only
explain: AWS X-Ray provides distributed tracing by instrumenting applications with the X-Ray SDK or daemon, creating a trace map showing request flow, service dependencies, response times, and errors across all microservices. Unlike CloudWatch (infrastructure metrics), X-Ray provides application-level request performance insights.

q: A company manages containers and needs orchestration. What AWS service provides this?
options: Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service) or EKS (Kubernetes) for orchestration
EC2 instances only
S3 for container images
Lambda for all container workloads
explain: Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service) is AWS's native container orchestration service for running Docker containers. Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) runs standard Kubernetes. Both handle deployment, health monitoring, scaling, and service discovery — choose ECS for AWS-native simplicity or EKS for Kubernetes portability.

q: A startup uses containers but wants to avoid managing infrastructure. Which service runs containers without managing servers?
options: AWS Fargate — serverless containers using ECS or EKS
Amazon RDS
S3 for container storage
EC2 instances directly
explain: AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers that eliminates the need to provision or manage EC2 instances as cluster nodes. You define your container's CPU and memory requirements; Fargate launches, runs, and scales the underlying infrastructure automatically, charging per vCPU-second and GB-second used.

q: A company wants to store container images securely. Which AWS service is a container registry?
options: S3 for all images
Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) — manages private images with built-in scanning
EC2 instances
CloudFront for images
explain: Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) is a fully managed Docker container registry that stores, manages, and scans container images for vulnerabilities. It integrates natively with ECS, EKS, and CodePipeline for automated deployments, and uses IAM for fine-grained access control to image repositories.

q: A company processes high-volume data in batches. Which service is optimized for this?
options: CloudWatch for batch processing
EC2 instances only
AWS Batch — fully managed batch computing with automatic scaling
Lambda for all batch jobs
explain: AWS Batch manages the entire batch computing lifecycle: provisioning optimal compute (EC2, Spot, Fargate), scheduling jobs in queues, managing job dependencies, and scaling resources. Unlike Lambda (15-minute max duration), Batch supports long-running jobs with no time limit and handles terabytes of data processing.

q: A company with variable workloads wants flexibility without long-term commitments. Which pricing model fits?
options: Spot Instances with interruption risk
Fixed pricing regardless of usage
On-Demand pricing — pay per hour/second with flexibility to scale
Reserved Instances requiring commitments
explain: On-Demand pricing charges for EC2 by the second (Linux) or hour (Windows) with no upfront fees or minimum commitments — maximum flexibility. This is appropriate for variable workloads, testing, and short-term needs. Reserved Instances and Savings Plans reduce costs for predictable workloads with 1-3 year commitments.

q: A company commits to a steady application for 3 years. What pricing model offers maximum savings?
options: 3-year Reserved Instances for 60-72% discount
Pay-as-you-go without analysis
On-Demand for flexibility
Spot Instances for interruption risk
explain: 3-year Reserved Instances with all-upfront payment provide 60-72% discount versus On-Demand pricing, offering the maximum savings for steady-state workloads. The 3-year term maximizes savings but reduces flexibility. Spot Instances offer deeper discounts but risk interruption for critical continuous workloads.

q: A startup wants to reduce EC2 costs and tolerates interruptions. What pricing option is ideal?
options: No cost optimization
Spot Instances — spare capacity at 70-90% discount, usable if interruptions are tolerable
Reserved Instances
On-Demand pricing
explain: EC2 Spot Instances purchase spare AWS capacity at 70-90% discount, but AWS reclaims them with a 2-minute warning when capacity is needed elsewhere. For fault-tolerant batch workloads that can save state and restart, Spot Instances provide the deepest discounts — not suitable for applications requiring continuous uninterrupted operation.

q: Which AWS service provides a petabyte-scale data warehouse for complex analytical queries across large datasets?
options: Amazon RDS for relational database workloads
Amazon Aurora for high-performance relational analytics
Amazon DynamoDB for high-speed NoSQL analytical queries
Amazon Redshift — managed petabyte-scale data warehouse using columnar storage
explain: Amazon Redshift is a fully managed data warehouse using columnar storage and massively parallel processing (MPP) to query petabytes of structured and semi-structured data with standard SQL. Unlike DynamoDB (NoSQL, for fast single-record lookups), Redshift is optimized for complex analytical aggregations across billions of rows.

q: A company wants reduced costs without capacity commitment. What combines savings plans with flexibility?
options: Reserved Instances
On-Demand pricing only
No flexible savings
AWS Savings Plans — hourly commitment discounts (up to 66%) across instance families and services
explain: AWS Savings Plans commit to a consistent hourly compute spend ($x/hour) for 1 or 3 years, automatically applying discounts (20-66%) to any EC2 instance type, family, size, and OS. Unlike Reserved Instances, Savings Plans don't lock you to a specific instance type — they apply automatically to your highest On-Demand usage.

q: A startup launches on AWS and wants to minimize first-year costs. What is the AWS Free Tier?
options: 12-month free tier, always-free services, and trials
AWS is always paid
Free tier is not available
All AWS services are free indefinitely
explain: AWS Free Tier includes three categories: 12-month free (EC2 t2.micro 750 hours/month, RDS 750 hours/month, S3 5GB), always-free (Lambda 1M requests/month, DynamoDB 25GB storage), and short-term trials. Usage beyond Free Tier limits is charged at standard rates — monitoring is essential to avoid unexpected charges.

q: A company needs to forecast monthly costs before incurring them. Which AWS tool provides estimates?
options: AWS CloudWatch for monitoring only
AWS Cost Explorer for historical analysis only
AWS Pricing Calculator — estimates costs based on selected services
No cost estimation tools available
explain: AWS Pricing Calculator (calculator.aws.amazon.com) estimates monthly costs by selecting services, configuring usage parameters, and choosing pricing models — before deploying any resources. Cost Explorer analyzes historical spending on existing resources; Pricing Calculator projects future costs for planned workloads.

q: A company's AWS costs are increasing unexpectedly. Which service analyzes spending and identifies drivers?
options: AWS Budgets for cost limits only
AWS Cost Explorer — visualizes spending, identifies drivers, and forecasts
CloudTrail for API logging only
AWS CloudWatch for metrics only
explain: AWS Cost Explorer visualizes spending with interactive graphs filterable by service, region, linked account, usage type, and cost allocation tags. It identifies which services drove unexpected cost increases, shows month-over-month trends, and forecasts future spend based on historical patterns.

q: A company wants alerts when AWS spending approaches a budget. Which AWS service provides this?
options: No budget alerting available
CloudWatch alarms for infrastructure metrics only
Cost Explorer for analysis only
AWS Budgets — sets cost/usage limits and triggers alerts
explain: AWS Budgets sets cost and usage thresholds with SNS notifications (email or alert) when actual spending reaches 50%, 80%, or 100% of the budget — or when forecasted costs are projected to exceed the budget. This proactive alerting prevents bill shock, unlike reviewing bills after charges are finalized.

q: A company compares TCO: on-premises versus AWS. What factors should analysis include?
options: Cloud is always cheaper
Only hardware cost
Only compute costs
Hardware, software, staffing (salaries), space, power, cooling, disaster recovery, and depreciation
explain: A complete TCO analysis includes: server hardware acquisition, software licenses, data center facility costs (space, power, cooling, security), IT staff salaries for infrastructure management, disaster recovery infrastructure, hardware refresh/depreciation cycles, and network equipment. AWS eliminates most of these categories.

q: What is the difference between an Application Load Balancer (ALB) and a Network Load Balancer (NLB)?
options: ALB operates at layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) enabling path-based routing; NLB operates at layer 4 (TCP/UDP) for ultra-high performance
ALB is for HTTPS traffic; NLB is for HTTP traffic only
ALB supports Auto Scaling; NLB requires manual scaling
NLB is newer and should replace ALB for all use cases
explain: ALB (Application Load Balancer) operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS), enabling path-based routing (/api → backend service, /static → S3), host-based routing, and WebSocket support — ideal for microservices. NLB (Network Load Balancer) at Layer 4 handles millions of TCP/UDP requests per second with static IP and ultra-low latency.

q: What are EC2 placement groups and when would you use a 'Cluster' placement group?
options: Spread placement groups placing instances on different hardware for fault tolerance
Cluster placement groups pack instances together in the same AZ for low-latency, high-throughput network performance for HPC
Groups of EC2 instances in different regions for global distribution
Groups of EC2 instances automatically distributed across AZs for high availability
explain: Cluster placement groups pack EC2 instances onto hardware in the same AZ and same rack to enable high-bandwidth, low-latency networking (25 Gbps) for HPC, big data analytics, and distributed computing. The trade-off is that all instances are in a single failure domain — use Spread placement groups for fault tolerance instead.

q: A company needs 24/7 support with sub-hour response times. Which AWS support plan is appropriate?
options: Business plan (1-hour critical response) or Enterprise plan (15-minute response)
Basic plan (free, limited)
Developer plan for paid support
Support requires additional service purchase
explain: AWS Business Support ($100/month minimum) provides 24/7 phone/chat with less than 1 hour response for production down. AWS Enterprise Support ($15,000/month minimum) provides less than 15 minutes for business-critical system down plus a dedicated TAM. Developer Support has no phone access and only business hours email.

q: A company consolidates multiple AWS accounts. What feature enables consolidated billing?
options: No consolidation available
Cost Explorer alone
AWS Organizations with consolidated billing and volume discounts
Individual bills for each account
explain: AWS Organizations consolidated billing combines all linked account charges into a single monthly bill from the management account. Usage aggregates across accounts — if account A uses 3TB of S3 and account B uses 4TB, combined 7TB qualifies for the higher-volume S3 tier pricing. SCPs provide additional governance.

q: AWS Trusted Advisor flags 'underutilized EC2 instances.' What does this recommendation suggest?
options: Instances at low utilization can be downsized or terminated to reduce costs
All instances must be fully utilized
No action needed
Underutilization is unavoidable
explain: AWS Trusted Advisor flags EC2 instances that have had consistently low CPU utilization (under 10%) for 14 days as potentially underutilized. Downsizing these instances to a smaller type or terminating unused instances directly reduces EC2 costs without impacting workloads.

q: An organization tracks costs by project/department. How can costs be allocated and tracked?
options: Manual spreadsheet tracking only
AWS doesn't support cost allocation
Only account-level billing available
Cost allocation tags — tag resources; Cost Explorer filters by tags
explain: Cost allocation tags are metadata applied to AWS resources (key-value pairs like Team=Backend, Environment=Production). After activating user-defined tags in Billing Preferences, they appear as filter dimensions in Cost Explorer and on the Cost and Usage Report for granular per-team or per-project cost breakdowns.

q: A company transfers large amounts of data out of AWS. They see high data transfer costs. How can these be reduced?
options: Data transfer is always expensive
AWS data transfer is free
AWS Direct Connect or reducing transfer frequency/volume
No way to optimize
explain: Data transfer OUT from AWS to the internet costs approximately $0.09/GB for the first 10TB. AWS Direct Connect can reduce this cost for large volumes by providing private connectivity with lower per-GB pricing. Amazon CloudFront reduces origin transfer by serving cached content from edge locations.

q: A company runs containerized applications and wants managed orchestration without managing cluster nodes. What AWS service provides this?
options: ECS with EC2 (requires managing instances)
AWS Fargate — serverless containers without managing cluster infrastructure
No managed container option
EC2 instances only
explain: AWS Fargate is serverless container compute that works with ECS or EKS to run containers without managing EC2 cluster nodes. You specify CPU and memory, and Fargate provisions the underlying infrastructure, handles patching, and scales capacity. You pay per vCPU-second and GB-second consumed by running containers.

q: Which AWS service provides a fully managed GraphQL API that connects to DynamoDB, Lambda, and other data sources?
options: Amazon Cognito for API authentication and authorization
Amazon API Gateway for RESTful API management
AWS Amplify for full-stack mobile and web application development
AWS AppSync for managed GraphQL APIs with real-time subscriptions and offline data sync
explain: AWS AppSync creates managed GraphQL APIs that aggregate data from multiple backends (DynamoDB, Lambda, HTTP endpoints, RDS) into a single unified API. It supports real-time data subscriptions (WebSocket connections) and offline data sync for mobile applications — features not available in REST-based API Gateway.

q: An application needs to process files uploaded to S3. What is the recommended serverless approach?
options: Polling S3 continuously with EC2
Manual file processing
Files cannot trigger automated processing
S3 event notifications triggering Lambda functions to process files
explain: S3 event notifications can trigger Lambda functions, SNS topics, or SQS queues when objects are created, modified, or deleted. This enables serverless, event-driven file processing: an uploaded image triggers a Lambda function to resize it and store thumbnails — no polling or dedicated servers required.

q: A company runs batch jobs processing terabytes of data nightly. Which AWS service is most cost-effective?
options: AWS Batch — scales compute automatically for batch processing
Lambda for all batch workloads
Running EC2 instances 24/7
Manual server management
explain: AWS Batch automatically provisions the right quantity and type of compute resources (EC2, Spot, Fargate) based on job volume and requirements, then terminates resources when jobs complete. This is more cost-effective than running EC2 24/7 for periodic batch work and more scalable than Lambda for long-running jobs.

q: A team wants to visualize application performance and correlate it with infrastructure metrics. Which AWS service provides this?
options: No visualization available
AWS CloudWatch — provides metrics, dashboards, and correlates infrastructure with application performance
CloudTrail for API logging only
Route 53 for DNS only
explain: Amazon CloudWatch collects and aggregates metrics from AWS services (CPU utilization, network I/O, disk metrics) and custom application metrics. It creates dashboards correlating infrastructure metrics with application performance metrics, enabling root cause analysis when performance degrades.

q: A company deploys a microservices application requiring service discovery and load balancing. What does a service mesh or AWS service provide?
options: Service Mesh only
AWS App Mesh or Elastic Load Balancing for service discovery and traffic distribution
Manual configuration only
No AWS service for this
explain: AWS App Mesh provides a service mesh for microservices, implementing service discovery and intelligent traffic routing using Envoy proxies. AWS Elastic Load Balancing distributes traffic across healthy targets. Both enable load-balanced communication between microservices without hardcoded service locations.

q: A company reviews their AWS bill and sees charges for data transfer out to the internet. How can they reduce this?
options: AWS Direct Connect reduces transfer costs; caching via CloudFront reduces origin bandwidth
No optimization possible
Data transfer is unavoidable
Only paying upfront eliminates transfer costs
explain: AWS Direct Connect provides private connectivity with lower per-GB data transfer pricing than internet-based transfer. Amazon CloudFront reduces origin data transfer costs by caching content at edge locations — users download from CloudFront edges rather than from the origin S3 or EC2 infrastructure.

q: A startup wants to forecast costs for a new project before launch. What AWS tool helps with this?
options: Only historical data is available
No forecasting tools exist
AWS Pricing Calculator — estimates costs based on service configurations
AWS Trusted Advisor only
explain: AWS Pricing Calculator (calculator.aws.amazon.com) estimates monthly costs for proposed workloads by configuring service parameters (instance types, storage sizes, data transfer volumes) before deploying anything. Cost Explorer provides analysis of existing charges — it cannot estimate costs for resources that don't yet exist.

q: A company uses AWS Organizations for consolidated billing. What additional benefit does Organizations provide?
options: Only consolidates bills
Requires separate AWS accounts elsewhere
No other benefits
Organizations enables consolidated billing, volume discounts, unified SCPs, and centralized cost visibility
explain: AWS Organizations provides consolidated billing, volume discount aggregation, Reserved Instance and Savings Plan sharing across linked accounts, Service Control Policies for governance, and AWS Control Tower integration. The management account receives a single invoice covering all linked member accounts.

q: An organization wants to implement chargeback for departmental cloud costs. How should they approach this?
options: Cloud costs cannot be allocated by department
Tag resources with cost-center/project; use Cost Explorer to filter and allocate costs to departments
No chargeback mechanism exists
Manual spreadsheets only
explain: Cost allocation tagging enables chargeback: tag every resource with department=engineering or cost-center=1234 tags. After activating tags in Billing Preferences, filter Cost Explorer or the Cost and Usage Report by these tags to show exact spending per department, enabling accurate internal billing.

q: A company commits $100,000/month in AWS spending. How should they purchase compute to optimize costs?
options: Spot Instances for all workloads
No cost optimization
On-Demand pricing for flexibility
Savings Plans committing to $/hour across instance families and services for 20-66% savings
explain: AWS Savings Plans commit to a consistent hourly compute spend (e.g., $10/hr) for 1 or 3 years, applying discounts automatically to the highest On-Demand usage. Unlike Reserved Instances that lock to specific attributes, Savings Plans apply to any EC2 instance type, region, OS, or tenancy — and also cover Lambda and Fargate.

q: A startup wants to minimize costs and accept that some workloads may be interrupted. What pricing model is best?
options: Spot Instances for up to 90% discount on spare capacity
Reserved Instances for all
Pay-as-you-go
On-Demand pricing only
explain: EC2 Spot Instances offer up to 90% discount on spare AWS capacity but can be reclaimed with 2-minute warning when AWS needs the capacity back. They are ideal for fault-tolerant batch processing, data analysis, rendering, and testing workloads that can checkpoint progress and restart after interruption.

q: A company's AWS costs increased unexpectedly. They need to identify the cause quickly. Which AWS tool provides this?
options: Manual analysis only
AWS Trusted Advisor for cost optimization only
CloudTrail for API logging only
AWS Cost Explorer — shows spending by service, identifies anomalies and cost drivers
explain: AWS Cost Explorer breaks down costs by service, region, usage type, linked account, and cost allocation tags in interactive visualizations. When costs spike unexpectedly, drilling into Cost Explorer by service and then by usage type reveals the specific resource type and region driving the increase.

q: What is the difference between Standard Reserved Instances and Convertible Reserved Instances?
options: Standard RIs are monthly commitments; Convertible RIs are annual commitments
Standard RIs offer higher discounts (up to 72%) but cannot change attributes; Convertible RIs allow changing instance family/OS/tenancy with slightly lower discounts (up to 66%)
Standard RIs can be sold on the marketplace; Convertible RIs cannot
Convertible RIs are more expensive with no additional flexibility benefits
explain: Standard Reserved Instances provide the highest discount (up to 72% off On-Demand) for 1 or 3-year commitments but cannot change instance type, OS, or tenancy. Convertible Reserved Instances offer slightly lower discount (up to 66%) but allow exchanges for different attributes during the term.

q: A company wants to prevent budget overruns by alerting teams when costs exceed limits. Which AWS service helps?
options: AWS CloudWatch for infrastructure metrics only
Cost Explorer for analysis only
No budget alerting available
AWS Budgets — sets cost/usage limits and triggers SNS notifications when approaching thresholds
explain: AWS Budgets creates custom thresholds and sends proactive SNS notifications when actual or forecasted spending approaches or exceeds defined limits. This enables teams to take corrective action — scaling down unused resources or requesting budget increases — before overspending occurs.

q: A company evaluates AWS for 5-year planning. They want the lowest possible cost for a long-term commitment. What should they purchase?
options: Spot Instances for long-term
5-year Savings Plans provide deeper discounts than 1-year (if available)
On-Demand pricing for flexibility
Reserved Instances for maximum savings
explain: Reserved Instances with 3-year all-upfront commitment provide the maximum discount (up to 72%) for long-term steady-state workloads. Savings Plans are more flexible but offer slightly lower maximum discounts. Spot Instances provide deeper discounts but are unsuitable for long-term production commitments.

q: A company reviews AWS security and wants to understand encryption key management. Which service controls encryption keys?
options: AWS KMS (Key Management Service) — manages encryption keys with IAM policies and audit logging
No key management service
S3 for key storage
IAM only for key management
explain: AWS KMS (Key Management Service) creates and manages Customer Managed Keys (CMKs) used to encrypt data at rest across S3, RDS, EBS, DynamoDB, and other services. KMS provides IAM-based access control to keys and integrates with CloudTrail to log every key usage for compliance auditing.

q: What is the difference between AWS Budgets alerts and CloudWatch billing alarms?
options: CloudWatch billing alarms are more sophisticated than AWS Budgets
They are identical services with different names in different AWS console locations
AWS Budgets monitors actual and forecasted costs with granular filtering; CloudWatch billing alarms only trigger when actual charges exceed a threshold
AWS Budgets is for enterprises; CloudWatch billing alarms are for individual developers
explain: AWS Budgets can send alerts on BOTH actual charges (already incurred) and FORECASTED charges (projected based on current usage trajectory). CloudWatch billing alarms only trigger on actual charges. This forecasting capability lets teams act before they exceed budgets rather than discovering overages after month-end.

q: A company wants to prevent accidental deletion of critical AWS resources. What service helps enforce this?
options: No prevention mechanism exists
Security Groups prevent deletion
Deletion is always allowed
AWS Config enforces compliance rules; IAM policies with Deny statements prevent specific actions
explain: AWS Config Rules evaluate resource configurations against compliance policies and flag non-compliant resources. IAM policies with explicit Deny statements prevent specific actions (like deleting specific DynamoDB tables or S3 buckets). Together, these services detect and prevent unauthorized changes to critical resources.

q: A company runs an ecommerce site requiring real-time inventory updates across regions. Which database service provides this?
options: S3 for inventory only
Amazon DynamoDB with global tables for multi-region replication
No real-time option
RDS in single region only
explain: Amazon DynamoDB Global Tables provide fully managed multi-region, multi-active replication with automatic conflict resolution. All replica tables support reads and writes with single-digit millisecond latency, automatically synchronizing data changes across regions — ideal for globally distributed e-commerce inventory.

q: What is Compute Savings Plans and how does it differ from EC2 Instance Savings Plans?
options: Compute Savings Plans apply to EC2, Fargate, and Lambda across any region/family/OS; EC2 Instance Savings Plans offer higher discounts but are restricted to specific EC2 family and region
They have identical discounts but different service coverage
EC2 Instance Savings Plans are purchased annually; Compute Savings Plans monthly
Compute Savings Plans apply only to EC2; EC2 Instance Savings Plans apply to all compute
explain: Compute Savings Plans (up to 66% off) apply to any EC2 instance type, size, region, OS, and tenancy, plus Lambda and Fargate. EC2 Instance Savings Plans (up to 72% off) offer higher discounts but are restricted to a specific EC2 instance family and region. Compute Savings Plans are the most flexible commitment option.

q: A company wants to cache frequently accessed database queries for performance. What AWS service enables this?
options: S3 for caching only
Amazon ElastiCache (Redis, Memcached) — caches data for low-latency access
No caching service
RDS caching only
explain: Amazon ElastiCache provides managed Redis or Memcached in-memory caching. Frequently queried database results are cached in ElastiCache (sub-millisecond latency), reducing database read load and dramatically improving application response times for read-heavy workloads.

q: Which AWS service helps detect and protect sensitive data like credit card numbers and SSNs stored in S3?
options: Amazon GuardDuty for detecting unauthorized S3 access attempts
AWS Config checking S3 bucket encryption settings
Amazon Inspector scanning S3 objects for security vulnerabilities
Amazon Macie using ML to discover and classify sensitive data in S3
explain: Amazon Macie uses ML models trained on common sensitive data patterns to automatically discover PII (SSNs, credit card numbers, driver's license numbers), financial data, and credentials in S3. GuardDuty detects who is accessing S3 abnormally, but doesn't classify the content of individual objects.

q: An application needs a message queue for asynchronous communication. Which AWS service provides this?
options: Amazon SQS (Simple Queue Service) — managed message queues for decoupled communication
S3 for messages
No queue service available
Direct API calls only
explain: Amazon SQS provides managed, durable message queues that store messages until consumers successfully process and delete them. Producers and consumers operate independently — if the consumer service is unavailable, messages queue up and process when the service recovers, enabling resilient asynchronous communication.

q: A company wants to give an EC2 instance permission to write to an S3 bucket securely without storing credentials on the instance. What is the best approach?
options: Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instance with S3 write permissions — temporary credentials are automatically provided
Create an IAM user and store credentials in Parameter Store on the instance
Use the root account credentials in the application configuration
Store IAM user access keys in a .env file on the EC2 instance
explain: IAM roles attached to EC2 instances provide temporary, automatically rotated credentials via the instance metadata service (IMDS). The AWS SDK automatically retrieves these credentials — no access keys are stored in code, configuration files, or environment variables, eliminating the most common credential exposure risk.

q: A company wants to stream real-time data (click events, transactions). Which AWS service handles streaming?
options: No streaming service
Amazon Kinesis — collects, processes, and analyzes streaming data at scale
S3 for streaming only
CloudFront for streaming only
explain: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams captures streaming data (clicks, transactions, sensor readings) with sub-second latency, retaining it for 1-365 days for multiple consumers to process concurrently. Kinesis Data Analytics enables real-time SQL or Apache Flink analysis; Kinesis Firehose delivers data to S3, Redshift, or OpenSearch.

q: A development team wants to deploy their application with infrastructure as code. Which AWS service provides this?
options: No infrastructure-as-code option
AWS CloudFormation — provisions infrastructure using declarative templates (JSON/YAML)
S3 for storing code only
EC2 manual configuration only
explain: AWS CloudFormation uses JSON or YAML templates to declare AWS resources and their configurations. Templates are version-controlled, enabling infrastructure change review, rollback to previous versions, drift detection, and consistent re-deployment across environments (dev, staging, production) from a single template.

q: What is the principle of 'immutable infrastructure' and how does it improve security?
options: Using hardware security modules that cannot be tampered with physically
Locking IAM permissions so they cannot be modified after creation
Replacing servers rather than patching them in-place — ensures known good state, eliminates configuration drift and persistent access
Infrastructure that cannot be modified or scaled after initial deployment
